Chapter 1: Phase 1 Summary and Objectives
1.1 Phase 1 Deliverable Definition
Phase 1 of MattBot delivers a mobile-first AI call concierge system that answers inbound calls, screens callers with a structured conversation, and produces actionable call records for the user. Phase 1 is explicitly defined as Option C and includes:
* Mobile application (iOS and Android)
* Backend services
* Inbound AI call handling
* Conditional call forwarding mode (required)
* Live handoff to the user (required)
Phase 1 is designed as a narrow “phone-first” release. Features and integrations not defined in the Phase 1 Master Plan are out of scope and must not be implemented or assumed in this phase.
1.1.1 Target User and Launch Parameters
Phase 1 is built for:
* Personal users only
* US-only launch market
* English-only language support
* Auto-detected timezone per user
Early usage assumptions for sizing and operational planning:
* 50 to 100 users (or fewer) in the first three months after launch
* Low to moderate concurrency, but the system must support bursts and abuse scenarios
Platform baseline:
* iOS and Android supported
* Minimum OS versions:
   * iOS 16+
   * Android 10+
Multi-device requirement:
* A single account can be logged in on multiple devices simultaneously
* Call history, settings, and lists must remain consistent across devices
* Device revocation must immediately remove access on the revoked device
1.1.2 Supported Call Entry Modes
Phase 1 supports two inbound call entry modes. Both modes must produce identical user outcomes (call record, transcript, summary, labels, notifications, and follow-up actions), differing only in how calls enter the system.
Mode A: Dedicated AI Number per User
* Each user receives a dedicated AI-managed phone number.
* Calls made directly to this number are answered by the AI.
* Number provisioning is automatic during onboarding.
* The user can share this number with callers as a primary contact path.
Mode B: Conditional Call Forwarding (Required)
* Users forward calls from their personal number to the AI number only when they are busy or unreachable.
* Forwarding setup must be guided in-app and include a verification step to confirm correct behavior.
* The system must support a forwarding test flow to validate:
   * AI does not answer when the user is available
   * AI does answer when the user is busy or unreachable
* Carrier and device variability must be handled through clear setup guidance and robust failure handling.
1.1.3 Inbound Call Handling Goals
When a call is answered by the AI (regardless of entry mode), the system must:
* Identify itself as an assistant acting on behalf of the user
* Run a short, structured screening conversation
* Capture sufficient information for the user to decide what to do next
* Enforce configured maximum call duration policies
* End calls politely and predictably
Minimum screening capture set:
* Caller identifier (name if provided; otherwise number)
* Reason for the call (one-line purpose)
* Urgency level
* Callback number confirmation (when applicable)
* Best callback time suggestion (prompted; optional if caller refuses)
Required special-case behaviors:
* VIP callers: prioritized handling and “Important” classification based on user configuration
* Blocked callers: consistent low-noise handling with appropriate logging rules
* Unknown callers: structured screening focused on identity and purpose
* Sales calls: label and handle quickly while still capturing the essential message
1.1.4 Call Record Outputs (Post-Call Artifacts)
Every handled call must produce a durable call record. A call record must be created even if downstream processing partially fails. The call record is the primary user-facing artifact that connects screening to follow-up actions.
Required call record components:
* Call metadata: timestamps, duration, entry mode (dedicated vs forwarded), and processing status
* Caller display: number and any caller-provided name
* Summary: concise, structured, and optimized for fast reading
* Transcript: speaker-separated text representation of the call
* Labels: VIP, urgent, sales, spam, normal, unknown, blocked (as applicable)
* Label explanations: reasons or signals used to assign labels where applicable
* Action history: record of callbacks, texts, notes, reminders, blocking actions, and label changes
* Live handoff timeline fields (if handoff was offered/attempted)
Failure-tolerance requirements:
* If transcript fails, the record must still exist and show the failure state
* If summary fails, the record must still exist with transcript and metadata
* If the call ends early, partial capture must be stored without guessing missing content
* Missing components must be clearly represented as missing, not silently omitted
Optional media:
* Call recordings are supported only if the user explicitly enables recording
* Recording is off by default
* If enabled, the assistant announces recording at call start
1.1.5 Follow-up Actions (Workflow Completion)
Phase 1 must allow the user to take action from each call record. Actions must be tracked and visible inside the call record.
Required actions:
* Call back
* Text back (approval-first)
* Add notes
* Create reminders
* Block caller
* Mark spam and correct labels
Texting rules:
* The system generates a draft message after each call by default
* Sending is always approval-first:
   * User must review and explicitly approve before sending
   * User may edit the draft before approval
* Message delivery status and failures must be recorded
* Retry behavior must exist for failed sends where appropriate
Reminder rules:
* One-time reminders are required in Phase 1
* Reminders must be linked to a call record and must trigger reliable notifications
1.1.6 Live Handoff (Takeover) Capability
Live handoff is included in Phase 1. The purpose of handoff is to allow the user to take over a call in real time when configured criteria are met.
Handoff configuration:
* Off
* VIP only
* Urgent only
* VIP or urgent
Default handoff trigger policy:
* VIP or urgent
Urgency handling policy:
* The assistant may classify urgency, but must confirm urgency with a short question before initiating handoff to reduce false positives.
Handoff experience:
* Handoff is offered after minimum context is captured (caller identifier and one-line reason)
* Handoff offer is delivered to all logged-in devices
* Only one device may accept (multi-device arbitration required)
* If not accepted within the timeout window, the assistant continues screening and captures a message
Handoff destination policy:
* Live handoff transfers the call to the user’s real phone number
Loop and failure safety:
* The system must prevent call forwarding loops during handoff attempts
* Only one handoff attempt is allowed per call
* If handoff fails due to no-answer, busy, voicemail detection, or loop detection, the assistant resumes message-taking and completes the call record
Voicemail handling during handoff:
* Voicemail detection is required
* If voicemail is detected for the user’s handoff destination, the system must stop the handoff attempt and return to the assistant flow immediately
1.1.7 Privacy and Security as MVP Scope
Phase 1 handles personal communications and must implement privacy and security as core deliverable requirements. Privacy and security controls are not optional and must be present at launch.
Core privacy behaviors:
* Notification privacy is private by default
   * Lock screen notifications show generic messages only
   * Sensitive content is visible only inside the app after user unlock
* Biometrics gating is required for sensitive content:
   * Call details
   * Transcript
   * Summary
   * Recordings
   * Memory view
Core data controls:
* Retention policies must be supported (7, 30, and 90 days)
* Default retention is 30 days
* Per-call deletion must delete all attached artifacts
* Delete-all-call-data must exist
* Account deletion must exist and revoke all sessions and devices
1.1.8 Admin Web Portal (Internal)
Phase 1 includes an internal admin web portal to support operations and troubleshooting.
Portal audience:
* Your team and Matt’s team only
* Not end users
Role-based access control:
* Admin: full control for support and operational tasks
* Support: operational support actions with restricted data and controls
* Viewer: read-only monitoring and system status access
Minimum portal capabilities:
* User management: search, status, device list, session revoke
* Call logs: visibility into call processing status and failures
* Forwarding diagnostics visibility
* Operational health: queue backlog, error trends, processing success rates
* Data deletion support tooling with audit trails
1.2 Definition of “DONE” for Phase 1
Phase 1 is considered done when the system meets the following acceptance outcomes and is stable for daily use.
1.2.1 Functional Acceptance Outcomes
A. Onboarding completion
* User can sign up and complete onboarding without manual support
* Dedicated number is provisioned automatically
* Forwarding setup guidance exists and can be verified with a test flow
* Notification registration succeeds and a test push can be delivered
* Biometrics and privacy defaults are configured during onboarding
B. Inbound call handling correctness
* Calls are answered reliably via dedicated number mode
* Calls are answered reliably via forwarding mode only when the user is busy or unreachable
* The system consistently produces correct call entry mode labeling for records
* The assistant captures required screening fields with predictable flow
C. Call record reliability
* Every handled call creates a call record
* Transcript and summary are produced for the majority of calls under normal conditions
* If components fail, the record still exists and shows missing states clearly
* Call records are consistent across devices
D. Follow-up actions correctness
* Callback works and is recorded
* Texting is always approval-first and cannot be sent without explicit approval
* Notes and reminders are saved and linked to calls
* Reminders reliably trigger notifications
* VIP and block list changes take effect in subsequent calls
E. Live handoff correctness
* Handoff triggers only under configured conditions
* Handoff offers are delivered to all devices
* Only one device can accept
* Handoff timeout and decline behavior are correct
* Loop prevention and failure fallback behaviors are correct
F. Privacy and security enforcement
* Notification privacy is private by default
* Biometrics gating protects sensitive call content
* Data is protected in transit and at rest
* Webhook validation and abuse controls exist
* Sensitive actions are audit logged without leaking call content
G. Data lifecycle correctness
* Retention policies work and are enforced consistently
* Per-call deletion removes all artifacts
* Delete-all-call-data works
* Account deletion works and revokes all sessions/devices
* Memory items can be viewed and deleted by the user
1.2.2 Operational Acceptance Outcomes
* Production deployment and rollback procedure exists
* Monitoring and alerting exist for critical failures:
   * call failures
   * transcript/summary failures
   * queue backlog
   * cost spikes
* Admin portal supports required operational workflows
1.2.3 Delivery Constraints
* Environments: development and production only
* Observability: self-hosted Sentry
* Timeline target: two months from the start date defined for Phase 1 delivery
1.3 Phase 1 Success Metrics
Phase 1 success is defined by user-visible outcomes, operational stability, and technical readiness to extend in later phases without rebuilding core systems.
1.3.1 User Experience Success Metrics
* Successful onboarding completion rate
* Forwarding setup success rate with verified behavior
* Call record usefulness:
   * summary is readable and accurate
   * transcript is available and coherent
* User action completion:
   * callbacks initiated
   * texts approved and sent
   * reminders created and triggered
* Reduced missed-call burden as reported by users
1.3.2 System Reliability Metrics
* Call answer rate
* Call completion rate
* Transcript success rate
* Summary success rate
* Notification delivery success rate
* Handoff offer rate and handoff success rate
* Retention deletion job success rate
* Outbound message send success rate
1.3.3 Operational and Cost Metrics
* Average processing time per call (end-to-end)
* Queue latency and backlog depth
* Cost per user per month (telephony, AI, storage)
* Rate-limit and abuse-trigger frequency
* Frequency of degraded-mode activation due to cap enforcement
1.3.4 Technical Readiness Metrics (No Rebuild Foundation)
* Identity and session model supports multi-device and future channels
* Settings model is versioned and extensible
* Call record model is structured and supports future conversion into a generalized conversation model
* Approval-first action pipeline can be reused for future outbound actions beyond SMS
* Notification event system is extensible for future event types
* Data lifecycle engine (retention and deletion) is reusable and verifiable
________________


Chapter 2: In Scope and Out of Scope
2.1 In Scope for Phase 1
Phase 1 scope is defined as a phone-first, inbound call concierge MVP for personal users. The scope includes all functionality required to reliably answer calls when the user is busy or unreachable, screen callers, produce actionable call records, and enable user-approved follow-ups, with privacy and security treated as required MVP features.
2.1.1 Deliverables Included in Phase 1
Phase 1 deliverables include:
1. Consumer Mobile App (iOS and Android)
   * User onboarding and setup flows
   * Call history and call details UI
   * Settings and personalization UI
   * Follow-up action workflows (call back, text approval, notes, reminders, blocking)
   * Privacy controls and biometric gating
2. Backend Services
   * Authentication, sessions, device management, and authorization
   * Call orchestration and call record persistence
   * AI pipeline orchestration for screening, transcription, summarization, and labeling
   * Notification dispatch and delivery tracking
   * Action pipeline for reminders and approval-first SMS
   * Data lifecycle controls (retention and deletion enforcement)
   * Abuse prevention controls (rate limits, blocking behavior)
3. Telephony and AI Provider Integrations
   * Twilio Voice for inbound calls
   * Twilio SMS for outbound texting
   * ElevenLabs for voice and speech-to-text
4. Internal Admin Web Portal
   * Internal-only operational and support tooling for the teams operating the system
   * Role-based access for internal users
5. Infrastructure and Operations
   * Production environment with secure networking and storage
   * Development environment
   * CI/CD automation for building and deploying backend and web portal
   * Monitoring, alerting, and incident readiness essentials
6. Testing, Verification, and Release Readiness
   * Test strategy and evidence requirements for core flows
   * App store release readiness steps for iOS and Android
   * Deployment and rollback procedures
2.1.2 Supported Users, Platforms, and Baseline Constraints
Phase 1 is in scope only for:
* Personal users
* US market
* English language
* Timezone auto-detection per user
* iOS and Android
* Multi-device login supported, with consistent data across devices
2.1.3 Inbound Calling Features Included
Phase 1 includes inbound call handling through two entry modes. Both modes must produce the same user-facing results (call record, transcript, summary, labels, notifications, and follow-ups).
A) Dedicated AI Number per User
In scope:
* Automatic provisioning of a dedicated AI number during onboarding
* Display of the AI number to the user for sharing
* Ability for the system to answer calls placed to this number
B) Conditional Call Forwarding Mode (Required)
In scope:
* Guided setup instructions for enabling carrier forwarding on the user’s phone number
* Support for conditional forwarding behaviors aligned to “busy and unreachable”
* Forwarding verification flow so users can confirm correct behavior
* Detection of common misconfiguration patterns (especially unconditional forwarding) and clear user guidance
2.1.4 Call Screening and Conversation Behavior Included
In scope:
* A structured screening conversation designed to capture only what is needed for follow-up
* Required information capture:
   * caller identifier (name if provided, otherwise number)
   * reason for call
   * urgency level
   * callback details (number confirmation and optionally a best callback time)
* Fallback behavior:
   * If the caller refuses to answer screening questions, the assistant switches to message-taking so the call still produces a usable result
* Assistant identity constraint:
   * The assistant must present itself as an assistant acting on behalf of the user and must not impersonate the user
2.1.5 Call Record Outputs Included
In scope for every handled call:
* Durable call record creation, even if parts of AI processing fail
* Call metadata including timestamps and duration
* Transcript generation and storage
* Summary generation and storage
* Label assignment and storage (for example VIP, urgent, spam, sales, normal, unknown)
* Label explanations where applicable so users can understand why a label was assigned
* Clear failure states when transcript or summary is missing, including retry support where appropriate
Optional, user-controlled:
* Call recording support is included, but recording is off by default and must be explicitly enabled by the user
* If recording is enabled, the assistant announces recording at the start of the call
Required:
* Voicemail detection is included in Phase 1
2.1.6 Follow-up Actions Included
In scope from the call record:
* Call back
* Text back using approval-first enforcement
* Add a note
* Create a reminder
* Block a caller
* Mark or correct labels (including spam)
Approval-first texting is in scope with strict enforcement:
* AI drafts a text after each call by default
* The user must review and approve before sending
* No attachments are required in Phase 1
* Delivery state and failures must be recorded, with retry handling for send failures where appropriate
Reminders in scope:
* One-time reminders
* Reminders must reliably notify the user
2.1.7 Live Handoff Included
Live handoff is included in Phase 1.
In scope:
* Configurable handoff enablement and trigger rules (off, VIP only, urgent only, VIP or urgent)
* Handoff offer delivered to all logged-in devices
* Arbitration so only one device can accept
* Timeout and fallback behavior:
   * If the user does not accept, declines, or is unreachable, the assistant continues screening and completes the call record
* Handoff destination for Phase 1:
   * Transfer to the user’s real phone number
* Loop prevention requirements:
   * The system must prevent forwarding loops during handoff attempts
   * Only one handoff attempt is permitted per call
   * On handoff failure, the system must resume assistant handling rather than re-offering handoff
2.1.8 Personalization and User Controls Included
In scope user settings include:
* Voice selection and tone presets
* Business hours and after-hours behavior
* Quiet hours for notification behavior
* VIP allow list and block list management
* Live handoff configuration
* Texting rules (approval-first always on)
* Recording on or off
* Retention duration selection
* Per-call deletion and delete-all controls
* Memory on or off, plus a memory management UI with delete controls
2.1.9 Privacy and Security Controls Included
Phase 1 includes privacy and security controls as mandatory scope.
In scope:
* Notification privacy is private by default (no sensitive content on lock screen)
* Biometric gating for sensitive screens and content
* Data protection in transit and at rest
* Strong access control to prevent cross-user exposure
* Audit logging for sensitive actions (device changes, deletions, outbound actions, and handoff events)
* Abuse prevention controls (rate limiting, blocking, cost containment mechanisms)
2.1.10 Internal Admin Web Portal Included
In scope:
* Internal-only portal for operational support and troubleshooting
* RBAC roles such as Admin, Support, Viewer
* Minimum operational capabilities:
   * user lookup and account status visibility
   * device and session visibility with revoke capability
   * call processing status visibility and failure diagnostics
   * basic operational health indicators relevant to call handling and post-call processing
The portal is not intended as a consumer-facing product feature in Phase 1.
________________


2.2 Out of Scope for Phase 1
Phase 1 out-of-scope is strict. Any feature not explicitly described in the Phase 1 high-level plan is out of scope, even if it appears valuable. This rule prevents scope creep and protects delivery within the two-month timeline.
2.2.1 Explicitly Out of Scope Items
The following categories are explicitly out of scope for Phase 1:
1. Inbox management for email and messaging channels
2. Social media integrations
3. Link and document reading features
4. Full automation that takes actions without user approval
5. Deep always-learning browsing-based memory of user online activity
6. Complex subagent orchestration beyond the call concierge workflow
7. Web3 or on-chain functionality
2.2.2 Additional Examples That Must Not Be Included Unless the High-Level Plan Is Updated
The following are common requests that must be treated as out of scope for Phase 1 unless the high-level Phase 1 plan is formally updated:
* Outbound sales campaigns and outbound cold calling workflows
* Appointment booking automation and calendar scheduling integrations
* CRM synchronization and lead management integrations
* WhatsApp or other consumer messaging channels
* Consumer web dashboard for end users
* Advanced contact syncing and enrichment beyond Phase 1 lists (VIP and block lists)
* Background personal assistant behaviors that act proactively without user initiation and approval
2.2.3 Non-Goals for Phase 1
The following are non-goals for Phase 1:
* Replacing carrier voicemail systems
* Achieving full carrier compatibility for forwarding without user setup variability
* Maximizing automation over user control
* Storing or learning sensitive personal behavior over time beyond explicitly user-controlled memory
________________


2.3 Phase 2 and Later Placeholders
Phase 1 must establish foundations that make later phases possible without rebuilding core systems. However, Phase 2 and later features must not be designed or implemented in Phase 1 beyond the minimum foundations described below.
2.3.1 Phase B Placeholder (Channels Expansion)
Future scope placeholder:
* Expand communication channels beyond calls
* Add email and one messaging channel
* Maintain draft-first and approval-first outbound action patterns
No detailed design is included in Phase 1 for these items.
2.3.2 Phase C Placeholder (Reading and Decision Support)
Future scope placeholder:
* Summarize user-provided links and files
* Discuss and organize knowledge with explicit user control
* Expand preference storage under strict visibility and deletion controls
No detailed design is included in Phase 1 for these items.
2.3.3 Foundations Phase 1 Must Establish for Future Phases
Phase 1 must include foundational architecture elements that are in scope as enabling work:
* Identity and permissions model that supports multi-device and strict isolation
* Versioned settings model that can expand to additional channels
* Event and notification framework extensible to new event types
* Data lifecycle engine (retention, deletion, memory controls) reusable for future data types
* Integration pattern that supports adding future connectors without redesign
________________


Chapter 3: Stakeholders, Roles, and Responsibilities
3.1 Stakeholder Map
Phase 1 has a small number of stakeholder groups, but each one has different access needs and different definitions of “success.” This chapter defines who the stakeholders are, what they need, and who owns each decision area so delivery does not stall and production is operated safely.
3.1.1 End users (personal users)
End users are personal users located in the United States using iOS or Android. They interact only with:
* The mobile app
* Their inbound call experience (dedicated AI number and forwarding)
* Notifications, call records, transcripts, summaries
* Follow-up actions (call back, approval-first text, notes, reminders, block/spam)
* Privacy and security controls (biometrics, retention, deletion, memory)
End users do not have access to the admin portal and do not interact with backend configuration directly.
3.1.2 Product owner and acceptance owner
The acceptance owner is the person who signs off that Phase 1 is “done” and approves go-live. This role owns:
* Scope enforcement (what is in Phase 1, what is out)
* Priority decisions when time is limited
* Final approval for release to production and app stores
* Approval of any changes that affect privacy defaults, retention, deletion, or security posture
3.1.3 Engineering delivery team
Engineering builds and maintains:
* Mobile app
* Admin web portal (internal)
* Backend services (core API, async jobs, notifications, retention/deletion engine)
* Realtime call handling service (telephony and media bridge)
* Infrastructure deployment and runtime configuration
Engineering also owns implementation details and technical quality bars:
* Correctness, reliability, performance
* Safe rollout and rollback
* Observability, alerts, and runbooks
* Secure handling of secrets and personal data
3.1.4 QA and verification
QA owns:
* The Phase 1 test plan and test report
* Evidence collection for critical user flows
* Regression coverage for telephony flows, privacy defaults, deletion correctness, and multi-device behavior
* Release readiness testing for app store builds and production deployments
QA is the gatekeeper for “tested and verified,” but does not override the acceptance owner.
3.1.5 Security reviewer
Security review is required because the system handles personal data and communications. Security review owns:
* Verification that cross-user access is impossible
* Verification that privacy defaults are correct and enforced
* Verification that logs do not leak sensitive call content
* Verification that secrets handling and transport encryption meet baseline requirements
* Verification that retention and deletion work as promised
The security reviewer can block release if core privacy or isolation checks fail.
3.1.6 Operations and support (internal)
Operations and support own:
* Monitoring dashboards and alert ownership
* Incident response coordination
* Admin portal usage for troubleshooting
* Forwarding setup support for users (through documented guidance and diagnostics)
* Cost monitoring and protection actions (rate limits, disabling high-cost features if needed)
Operations access must be controlled tightly because the admin portal can expose sensitive metadata.
3.1.7 External service providers
External vendors are not stakeholders in decision-making, but they are dependencies that shape reliability and failure modes:
* Telephony and SMS provider
* AI voice and speech-to-text provider
* Cloud provider for compute, database, queues, storage
* Push notification networks (Apple and Google)
Engineering and operations own the integration contracts, monitoring, and fallback behaviors.
________________


3.2 Teams and Access Model
Phase 1 requires collaboration between two internal groups:
* Your team
* Matt’s team
This section defines access boundaries so work can proceed without overexposing production systems or personal data.
3.2.1 Access principles
All access follows these principles:
1. Least privilege
* Each person gets the minimum access required for their tasks.
2. Separation of duties
* The ability to deploy is separate from the ability to change production secrets or view sensitive user content.
* The ability to view call content is restricted to roles that require it for debugging and is audited.
3. Environment separation
* Development access does not automatically grant production access.
* Production access requires explicit approval and is time-bound where possible.
4. Traceability
* All privileged actions must be attributable to a named individual account.
* Shared credentials are not allowed.
3.2.2 Systems that require controlled access
Phase 1 involves the following systems that must have explicit access control and role assignments:
A) Source control and CI/CD
* Repository access (read, write, admin)
* GitHub Actions management
* Production deployment approvals and environment protections
B) Cloud infrastructure
* VM access (SSH) and container runtime access
* Database administrative access
* Queue administration access
* Storage administration access
* Network and firewall configuration access
C) Telephony and SMS provider console
* Number provisioning and configuration
* Webhook configuration
* Debugging call routing
* Reviewing delivery and error logs
D) AI provider console
* API key management
* Model configuration settings (if any)
* Usage monitoring
E) Observability and logging
* Self-hosted Sentry administration
* Alert rule configuration
* Issue triage and release health monitoring
F) App store accounts
* iOS distribution account access
* Android distribution account access
* Certificate and signing key management
G) Project management
* Asana project and task management
* Access for your team and Matt’s team
3.2.3 Team access responsibilities
Your team and Matt’s team must have:
* Access to the admin portal (appropriate roles only)
* Access to Asana for delivery tracking
* Engineering access aligned to actual responsibilities
Access must be granted per person, not per team, with role-based permissions.
________________


3.3 Internal Roles for the Admin Web Portal
The admin portal is internal-only and exists for operations, support, and troubleshooting. It must enforce role-based access control.
3.3.1 Portal role definitions
Portal roles are:
Admin
Admin is the highest-privilege portal role and should be limited to a small set of trusted operators.
Admin can:
* View user profiles and account status
* View device lists and revoke devices
* View call processing status and failure reasons
* View operational health dashboards (queues, job failures, error trends)
* Perform safe operational actions, including:
   * Triggering reprocessing jobs (summary retry)
   * Toggling operational feature flags for incident mitigation
   * Adjusting rate limit configuration within approved bounds
* Initiate user data deletion support actions only when required and audited
Admin cannot:
* Download or export bulk user data
* Bypass authentication or biometric protection rules in the consumer experience
Support
Support role exists for customer support and basic troubleshooting.
Support can:
* Look up a user by identifier and view account status
* View device list and request device revoke workflows
* View call record metadata and processing statuses
* View forwarding diagnostics indicators and common misconfiguration signals
* Guide users through forwarding setup using documented steps
Support cannot:
* View sensitive call content by default (transcript, summary, recording)
* Perform destructive actions like delete-all-call-data or account deletion
* Change feature flags or rate limits
If Support is allowed to view call content for debugging, it must be:
* Explicitly enabled
* Limited to a small subgroup
* Fully audited
* Used only when the user consents or when internal policy allows it
Viewer
Viewer is read-only and intended for stakeholders who need visibility without operational power.
Viewer can:
* View system health dashboards and aggregate metrics
* View high-level call processing success rates and failure trends
Viewer cannot:
* View user-specific data
* Perform any operational actions
3.3.2 Portal permission areas
Portal permissions must be implemented as explicit capability checks in these areas:
1. User management
* Search and view account state
* Session visibility
* Device revoke
2. Call operations
* Call processing pipeline status
* Failure reason visibility
* Handoff offer and acceptance logs
* Notification delivery status
3. Telephony diagnostics
* Forwarding verification indicators
* Webhook error visibility
* Provider-side incident flags (when available)
4. Data lifecycle tools
* Per-call deletion assistance
* Delete-all-call-data support actions
* Account deletion initiation and tracking
All destructive actions require admin permission and must be audit logged.
   5. Operational controls
   * Feature flags relevant to risk and cost control
   * Rate limit adjustments within approved constraints
   * Incident-mode toggles
   6. Audit visibility
   * Audit log search for privileged and destructive actions
   * Security-relevant event visibility
3.3.3 Audit requirements for portal actions
All portal actions that affect user data or system behavior must produce an audit event, including:
   * Device revoke
   * Retention setting changes performed by support tooling
   * Deletion actions
   * Feature flag toggles
   * Rate limit updates
   * Access to sensitive call content (if allowed at all)
________________


3.4 Delivery Team Roles and Responsibilities
Phase 1 delivery team composition is:
   * 2 fullstack developers
   * 1 QA
This section defines responsibilities so work does not overlap incorrectly and critical tasks are not missed.
3.4.1 Fullstack developer responsibilities
Each fullstack developer must be capable of working across mobile, backend, and web portal, but ownership boundaries still matter.
Core responsibilities across the two developers:
   * Implement mobile app flows and UI
   * Implement backend APIs and data model
   * Implement async processing jobs and retry logic
   * Implement realtime call event delivery to the app (websockets)
   * Implement admin portal screens and RBAC
   * Integrate telephony and AI services
   * Implement privacy defaults, biometric gating rules, and data lifecycle flows
   * Write unit tests and integration tests aligned to owned modules
   * Participate in code review and enforce quality gates
Recommended ownership split to reduce collisions:
   * Developer A: Mobile app + consumer experience foundations
   * Onboarding, call list, call details, actions, settings, privacy UX
   * Local secure storage and cache clearing rules
   * Mobile test harness and device validation support
   * Developer B: Backend + telephony/AI pipeline foundations
   * Core API, auth/session/device model
   * Call orchestration, job queues, transcript/summary pipeline
   * Admin portal backend endpoints
   * Operational controls (feature flags, rate limits)
   * Telephony routing logic and webhook validation
Both developers share responsibility for:
   * End-to-end integration across the call lifecycle
   * Performance and reliability fixes
   * Final hardening for production launch
3.4.2 QA responsibilities
QA responsibilities include:
   * Build the Phase 1 master test plan with test categories and evidence requirements
   * Verify end-to-end call flows:
   * dedicated AI number path
   * forwarding busy
   * forwarding unreachable
   * voicemail detection behavior
   * live handoff offer, accept, fail, and loop prevention
   * Verify privacy and security behaviors:
   * notification private mode default and leakage checks
   * biometric gating correctness
   * cross-user isolation checks
   * logging safety checks
   * Verify data lifecycle:
   * delete single call
   * delete all call data
   * retention expiration deletion
   * account deletion
   * multi-device sync after deletion
   * Produce a structured test report:
   * what was tested
   * pass/fail counts
   * critical issues
   * deferred items with risk and mitigation
   * known limitations, especially forwarding variability
   * evidence links
QA also validates that fixes are actually fixed through regression tests.
3.4.3 Acceptance owner responsibilities
The acceptance owner:
   * Defines final acceptance criteria interpretation for Phase 1 “done”
   * Approves what gets deferred if needed, only when privacy and safety are not weakened
   * Signs off on go/no-go after reviewing:
   * QA test report
   * engineering stability statement
   * security verification statement
   * production readiness checks and rollback plan
________________


3.5 Decision Ownership and Escalation Paths
Phase 1 involves fast decisions. The following ownership model prevents deadlocks.
3.5.1 Decision ownership areas
A) Scope and product tradeoffs
   * Owned by acceptance owner
B) Technical architecture and implementation details
   * Owned by engineering, with acceptance owner approval when it affects timeline, cost, or user privacy
C) Release readiness and quality gates
   * Owned by QA for test completion
   * Owned by engineering for deployability and stability
   * Owned by security reviewer for privacy and isolation
D) Production incident actions
   * Owned by operations lead or designated incident commander
   * Actions include disabling high-risk features via flags and tightening rate limits
3.5.2 Escalation path
When a decision blocks progress:
   1. Engineering proposes options with impact on timeline, risk, and user experience
   2. QA provides testability and risk notes
   3. Security reviewer provides privacy and isolation risk notes if applicable
   4. Acceptance owner chooses the final direction
________________


3.6 Required Collaboration Artifacts
To ensure both teams remain aligned and the technical plan remains implementable, Phase 1 requires the following artifacts and ownership:
3.6.1 Task tracking
   * Asana is the source of truth for tasks, sprint scope, and milestones.
   * Every engineering task must map to an acceptance outcome or required foundation.
3.6.2 Code review and merge rules
   * No direct pushes to the main branch.
   * Every change requires a pull request and review.
   * Security-sensitive changes require review by the designated security reviewer or a senior engineer acting in that role.
3.6.3 Release readiness package
Before go-live, the team must produce:
   * QA test report with evidence
   * Operational readiness checklist completion
   * Confirmed ownership for alerts and incident handling
   * Verified ability to disable risky or expensive features quickly through feature flags
   * Confirmed rollback steps for backend deployments
Chapter 3: Stakeholders, Roles, and Responsibilities
3.1 Stakeholder Map
Phase 1 has a small number of stakeholder groups, but each one has different access needs and different definitions of “success.” This chapter defines who the stakeholders are, what they need, and who owns each decision area so delivery does not stall and production is operated safely.
3.1.1 End users (personal users)
End users are personal users located in the United States using iOS or Android. They interact only with:
   * The mobile app
   * Their inbound call experience (dedicated AI number and forwarding)
   * Notifications, call records, transcripts, summaries
   * Follow-up actions (call back, approval-first text, notes, reminders, block/spam)
   * Privacy and security controls (biometrics, retention, deletion, memory)
End users do not have access to the admin portal and do not interact with backend configuration directly.
3.1.2 Product owner and acceptance owner
The acceptance owner is the person who signs off that Phase 1 is “done” and approves go-live. This role owns:
   * Scope enforcement (what is in Phase 1, what is out)
   * Priority decisions when time is limited
   * Final approval for release to production and app stores
   * Approval of any changes that affect privacy defaults, retention, deletion, or security posture
3.1.3 Engineering delivery team
Engineering builds and maintains:
   * Mobile app
   * Admin web portal (internal)
   * Backend services (core API, async jobs, notifications, retention/deletion engine)
   * Realtime call handling service (telephony and media bridge)
   * Infrastructure deployment and runtime configuration
Engineering also owns implementation details and technical quality bars:
   * Correctness, reliability, performance
   * Safe rollout and rollback
   * Observability, alerts, and runbooks
   * Secure handling of secrets and personal data
3.1.4 QA and verification
QA owns:
   * The Phase 1 test plan and test report
   * Evidence collection for critical user flows
   * Regression coverage for telephony flows, privacy defaults, deletion correctness, and multi-device behavior
   * Release readiness testing for app store builds and production deployments
QA is the gatekeeper for “tested and verified,” but does not override the acceptance owner.
3.1.5 Security reviewer
Security review is required because the system handles personal data and communications. Security review owns:
   * Verification that cross-user access is impossible
   * Verification that privacy defaults are correct and enforced
   * Verification that logs do not leak sensitive call content
   * Verification that secrets handling and transport encryption meet baseline requirements
   * Verification that retention and deletion work as promised
The security reviewer can block release if core privacy or isolation checks fail.
3.1.6 Operations and support (internal)
Operations and support own:
   * Monitoring dashboards and alert ownership
   * Incident response coordination
   * Admin portal usage for troubleshooting
   * Forwarding setup support for users (through documented guidance and diagnostics)
   * Cost monitoring and protection actions (rate limits, disabling high-cost features if needed)
Operations access must be controlled tightly because the admin portal can expose sensitive metadata.
3.1.7 External service providers
External vendors are not stakeholders in decision-making, but they are dependencies that shape reliability and failure modes:
   * Telephony and SMS provider
   * AI voice and speech-to-text provider
   * Cloud provider for compute, database, queues, storage
   * Push notification networks (Apple and Google)
Engineering and operations own the integration contracts, monitoring, and fallback behaviors.
________________


3.2 Teams and Access Model
Phase 1 requires collaboration between two internal groups:
   * Your team
   * Matt’s team
This section defines access boundaries so work can proceed without overexposing production systems or personal data.
3.2.1 Access principles
All access follows these principles:
   1. Least privilege
   * Each person gets the minimum access required for their tasks.
   2. Separation of duties
   * The ability to deploy is separate from the ability to change production secrets or view sensitive user content.
   * The ability to view call content is restricted to roles that require it for debugging and is audited.
   3. Environment separation
   * Development access does not automatically grant production access.
   * Production access requires explicit approval and is time-bound where possible.
   4. Traceability
   * All privileged actions must be attributable to a named individual account.
   * Shared credentials are not allowed.
3.2.2 Systems that require controlled access
Phase 1 involves the following systems that must have explicit access control and role assignments:
A) Source control and CI/CD
   * Repository access (read, write, admin)
   * GitHub Actions management
   * Production deployment approvals and environment protections
B) Cloud infrastructure
   * VM access (SSH) and container runtime access
   * Database administrative access
   * Queue administration access
   * Storage administration access
   * Network and firewall configuration access
C) Telephony and SMS provider console
   * Number provisioning and configuration
   * Webhook configuration
   * Debugging call routing
   * Reviewing delivery and error logs
D) AI provider console
   * API key management
   * Model configuration settings (if any)
   * Usage monitoring
E) Observability and logging
   * Self-hosted Sentry administration
   * Alert rule configuration
   * Issue triage and release health monitoring
F) App store accounts
   * iOS distribution account access
   * Android distribution account access
   * Certificate and signing key management
G) Project management
   * Asana project and task management
   * Access for your team and Matt’s team
3.2.3 Team access responsibilities
Your team and Matt’s team must have:
   * Access to the admin portal (appropriate roles only)
   * Access to Asana for delivery tracking
   * Engineering access aligned to actual responsibilities
Access must be granted per person, not per team, with role-based permissions.
________________


3.3 Internal Roles for the Admin Web Portal
The admin portal is internal-only and exists for operations, support, and troubleshooting. It must enforce role-based access control.
3.3.1 Portal role definitions
Portal roles are:
Admin
Admin is the highest-privilege portal role and should be limited to a small set of trusted operators.
Admin can:
   * View user profiles and account status
   * View device lists and revoke devices
   * View call processing status and failure reasons
   * View operational health dashboards (queues, job failures, error trends)
   * Perform safe operational actions, including:
   * Triggering reprocessing jobs (summary retry)
   * Toggling operational feature flags for incident mitigation
   * Adjusting rate limit configuration within approved bounds
   * Initiate user data deletion support actions only when required and audited
Admin cannot:
   * Download or export bulk user data
   * Bypass authentication or biometric protection rules in the consumer experience
Support
Support role exists for customer support and basic troubleshooting.
Support can:
   * Look up a user by identifier and view account status
   * View device list and request device revoke workflows
   * View call record metadata and processing statuses
   * View forwarding diagnostics indicators and common misconfiguration signals
   * Guide users through forwarding setup using documented steps
Support cannot:
   * View sensitive call content by default (transcript, summary, recording)
   * Perform destructive actions like delete-all-call-data or account deletion
   * Change feature flags or rate limits
If Support is allowed to view call content for debugging, it must be:
   * Explicitly enabled
   * Limited to a small subgroup
   * Fully audited
   * Used only when the user consents or when internal policy allows it
Viewer
Viewer is read-only and intended for stakeholders who need visibility without operational power.
Viewer can:
   * View system health dashboards and aggregate metrics
   * View high-level call processing success rates and failure trends
Viewer cannot:
   * View user-specific data
   * Perform any operational actions
3.3.2 Portal permission areas
Portal permissions must be implemented as explicit capability checks in these areas:
   1. User management
   * Search and view account state
   * Session visibility
   * Device revoke
   2. Call operations
   * Call processing pipeline status
   * Failure reason visibility
   * Handoff offer and acceptance logs
   * Notification delivery status
   3. Telephony diagnostics
   * Forwarding verification indicators
   * Webhook error visibility
   * Provider-side incident flags (when available)
   4. Data lifecycle tools
   * Per-call deletion assistance
   * Delete-all-call-data support actions
   * Account deletion initiation and tracking
All destructive actions require admin permission and must be audit logged.
      5. Operational controls
      * Feature flags relevant to risk and cost control
      * Rate limit adjustments within approved constraints
      * Incident-mode toggles
      6. Audit visibility
      * Audit log search for privileged and destructive actions
      * Security-relevant event visibility
3.3.3 Audit requirements for portal actions
All portal actions that affect user data or system behavior must produce an audit event, including:
      * Device revoke
      * Retention setting changes performed by support tooling
      * Deletion actions
      * Feature flag toggles
      * Rate limit updates
      * Access to sensitive call content (if allowed at all)
________________


3.4 Delivery Team Roles and Responsibilities
Phase 1 delivery team composition is:
      * 2 fullstack developers
      * 1 QA
This section defines responsibilities so work does not overlap incorrectly and critical tasks are not missed.
3.4.1 Fullstack developer responsibilities
Each fullstack developer must be capable of working across mobile, backend, and web portal, but ownership boundaries still matter.
Core responsibilities across the two developers:
      * Implement mobile app flows and UI
      * Implement backend APIs and data model
      * Implement async processing jobs and retry logic
      * Implement realtime call event delivery to the app (websockets)
      * Implement admin portal screens and RBAC
      * Integrate telephony and AI services
      * Implement privacy defaults, biometric gating rules, and data lifecycle flows
      * Write unit tests and integration tests aligned to owned modules
      * Participate in code review and enforce quality gates
Recommended ownership split to reduce collisions:
      * Developer A: Mobile app + consumer experience foundations
      * Onboarding, call list, call details, actions, settings, privacy UX
      * Local secure storage and cache clearing rules
      * Mobile test harness and device validation support
      * Developer B: Backend + telephony/AI pipeline foundations
      * Core API, auth/session/device model
      * Call orchestration, job queues, transcript/summary pipeline
      * Admin portal backend endpoints
      * Operational controls (feature flags, rate limits)
      * Telephony routing logic and webhook validation
Both developers share responsibility for:
      * End-to-end integration across the call lifecycle
      * Performance and reliability fixes
      * Final hardening for production launch
3.4.2 QA responsibilities
QA responsibilities include:
      * Build the Phase 1 master test plan with test categories and evidence requirements
      * Verify end-to-end call flows:
      * dedicated AI number path
      * forwarding busy
      * forwarding unreachable
      * voicemail detection behavior
      * live handoff offer, accept, fail, and loop prevention
      * Verify privacy and security behaviors:
      * notification private mode default and leakage checks
      * biometric gating correctness
      * cross-user isolation checks
      * logging safety checks
      * Verify data lifecycle:
      * delete single call
      * delete all call data
      * retention expiration deletion
      * account deletion
      * multi-device sync after deletion
      * Produce a structured test report:
      * what was tested
      * pass/fail counts
      * critical issues
      * deferred items with risk and mitigation
      * known limitations, especially forwarding variability
      * evidence links
QA also validates that fixes are actually fixed through regression tests.
3.4.3 Acceptance owner responsibilities
The acceptance owner:
      * Defines final acceptance criteria interpretation for Phase 1 “done”
      * Approves what gets deferred if needed, only when privacy and safety are not weakened
      * Signs off on go/no-go after reviewing:
      * QA test report
      * engineering stability statement
      * security verification statement
      * production readiness checks and rollback plan
________________

Phase 1 involves fast decisions. The following ownership model prevents deadlocks.
3.5.1 Decision ownership areas
A) Scope and product tradeoffs
      * Owned by acceptance owner
B) Technical architecture and implementation details
      * Owned by engineering, with acceptance owner approval when it affects timeline, cost, or user privacy
C) Release readiness and quality gates
      * Owned by QA for test completion
      * Owned by engineering for deployability and stability

D) Production incident actions
      * Owned by operations lead or designated incident commander

      2. QA provides testability and risk notes

      * Confirmed rollback steps for backend deployments
________________


Chapter 4: Assumptions and Constraints
This chapter defines the assumptions and hard constraints that Phase 1 must respect. These items shape architecture, testing, delivery sequencing, and the “no surprises” rules that keep the project shippable within the timebox.
4.1 Launch Parameters
4.1.1 Market, users, and languages
Phase 1 launch is constrained to:
      * Market: United States only
      * User type: Personal users only
      * Language: English only
      * Timezone behavior: Auto-detect per user
These choices reduce initial complexity in legal, support, and localization.
4.1.2 Expected early usage (sizing assumption)
Phase 1 is sized for an early cohort of:
      * 50 to 100 users (or fewer) in the first three months after launch
This is not a guarantee of low load. It is a planning baseline. The system must still handle bursts (for example: a spam caller flood hitting many users) without collapsing.
4.1.3 Problem framing assumptions (what Phase 1 is and is not trying to do)
Phase 1 assumes:
      * The goal is not to replace native voicemail at the operating system level.
      * The product delivers voicemail-like value through:
      * A dedicated AI number, and/or
      * Conditional call forwarding to AI when the user is busy or unreachable
Phase 1 also assumes:
      * Carrier forwarding behavior varies across carriers and devices.
      * Because of this, Phase 1 must include setup guidance and a verification flow so users can confirm their forwarding configuration behaves as intended.
4.1.4 Call flow assumptions that constrain the product experience
Phase 1 assumes the assistant:
      * Is polite and efficient
      * Collects the required fields every time
      * Avoids long conversations and ends calls cleanly once the goal is met
      * Never pretends to be the user and presents itself clearly as an assistant
      * Supports user control through settings, including call length and live handoff
Phase 1 call length defaults are constrained to:
      * Standard callers: maximum 3 minutes
      * VIP callers: maximum 5 minutes
The assistant should end earlier if the required fields are collected.
4.1.5 Legal and support complexity assumptions
Phase 1 assumes:
      * Some features add legal and support complexity, especially call recording and advanced voice personalization.
      * If recording is enabled, the assistant must announce recording at the start.
      * Calls being transcribed and summarized must be disclosed through a defined mechanism.
These assumptions force “safe defaults” and explicit user controls rather than hidden behavior.
________________


4.2 Platform Constraints
4.2.1 Supported platforms and minimum OS versions
Phase 1 must support:
      * iOS 16+
      * Android 10+
This constraint is intended to reduce fragmentation, simplify security posture, and improve reliability of push notifications and secure storage.
4.2.2 Multi-device behavior constraints
Phase 1 must support a single user being logged in on multiple devices at the same time.
This implies hard constraints on system behavior:
      * Settings changes must propagate across devices
      * Call records must appear consistently across devices
      * Session/device revocation must remove access quickly and reliably
      * Notifications must be able to fan out to all registered devices for the user
4.2.3 Device security capability assumptions
Phase 1 assumes modern device security primitives exist and must be used:
      * Secure storage for sensitive cached data (if any is cached)
      * Biometric gating for sensitive screens and content based on policy
      * Reliable push notification support across iOS and Android
________________


4.3 Environment Constraint
4.3.1 Environments allowed
Phase 1 is constrained to:
      * Development environment
      * Production environment
      * No staging environment
This is a hard constraint. It increases integration risk, especially around telephony and call routing. The technical plan must therefore include explicit mitigations to make this safe.
4.3.2 Required mitigations because staging is not available
Because Twilio integration and call routing are high-risk areas, Phase 1 must compensate for the lack of staging using the following mitigations:
      1. Strict environment separation
      * Development and production must use different credentials, webhooks, numbers, and storage paths.
      * No development traffic must ever reach production numbers or production webhooks.
      2. Production-safe rollout controls
      * Phase 1 must support operational switches that reduce risk without shipping a new app build.
      * Minimum controls must exist to disable risky or expensive paths quickly, including:
      * Recording on/off
      * Live handoff on/off
      * Text sending connector on/off
      * Advanced spam detection on/off
      * Summary retry on/off
      * Rate limits must be adjustable operationally.
      3. Verification and incident rehearsal in a non-production setup
      * Phase 1 requires a simple incident plan with a rehearsal (tabletop exercise) covering scenarios like:
      * Call failure spike
      * Notification outage
      * Cost spike from spam flood
      * Since staging is not available, this rehearsal must be executed using development infrastructure configured to mimic production behavior while using non-production numbers and credentials.
      4. Stronger automated testing requirements
      * Unit, integration, and end-to-end tests are required.
      * Telephony flows require special emphasis: dedicated number, forwarded busy/unreachable, voicemail detection, live handoff success/fail/loop prevention.
      5. Forwarding setup verification
      * The app must include a test flow that verifies forwarding behavior in real conditions.
      * The system must help detect common misconfigurations (especially unconditional forwarding) and guide the user to fix them.
4.3.3 Observability and monitoring constraints
Phase 1 must use:
      * Self-hosted Sentry for error tracking
Monitoring and alerts are mandatory and must include at least:
      * Failed calls
      * Failed summaries
      * Queue backlog
      * Cost spikes
These are not optional because telephony and AI failures are expected and must be visible immediately.
4.3.4 Secrets management constraint (cost-sensitive)
Phase 1 assumes a managed secrets service may be avoided due to cost. The constraint is:
      * Local development uses a local .env file that is never committed.
      * CI/CD uses GitHub Secrets for protected values.
      * Production secrets must be injected at deploy time and stored only on the server with strict OS permissions.
This constraint also implies:
      * No secrets embedded in mobile builds
      * No secrets stored in source control
      * Rotation and revocation must be possible without code changes
________________


4.4 Timeline Constraint
4.4.1 Delivery window
Phase 1 is constrained to an end-to-end delivery target of:
      * Two months after February 16, 2026
      * Target completion window: around April 16, 2026 (dependent on exact start alignment)
This is a timebox that drives prioritization and forces strict scope control.
4.4.2 Team capacity constraint
Phase 1 delivery capacity is constrained to:
      * 2 fullstack developers
      * 1 QA
      * Acceptance sign-off by the owner
This constraint means:
      * Work must be sequenced to minimize rework.
      * High-risk integrations (telephony, handoff, notification reliability, deletion correctness) must be addressed early.
      * Anything not explicitly in Phase 1 scope must not be added.
4.4.3 Scope stability rule (timebox protection)
Because the timebox is fixed:
      * Phase 1 scope must remain aligned to the high-level Phase 1 document.
      * Any changes that increase complexity must be treated as a schedule risk and require explicit acceptance-owner approval.
      * Deferrals are allowed only if the core call experience is stable and privacy is not weakened, with a mitigation such as a feature flag defaulting to off.
________________


CHAPTER 5: FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS CATALOG
This chapter is the master checklist of everything Phase 1 must do from a user and system behavior standpoint. These requirements define what the mobile app, backend, telephony integration, AI layer, notifications, and the internal admin portal must collectively deliver for Phase 1 to be considered complete.
5.0 Functional scope statement for Phase 1
Phase 1 must deliver an inbound call screening assistant for personal users with:
      * Dedicated AI number per user (callers can call the assistant line directly)
      * Conditional call forwarding mode (busy and unreachable only) so the AI answers only when the user cannot answer
      * Live handoff so the user can take over a call in real time when rules match
      * Call records with summary, transcript, labels, and follow-up actions
      * Approval-first texting with AI drafted messages that require user approval to send
      * Reminders, notes, VIP list, block list, spam labeling, and user-controlled retention and deletion
      * Internal web admin portal for support and operations (not for end users)
Anything not defined in the high-level Phase 1 documentation is out of scope by default.
________________


5.1 Onboarding and setup flows
5.1.1 Onboarding principles
Onboarding must be a guided checklist with clear progress. A user must be able to pause and resume without breaking setup. Onboarding must end with proof that the system works via an actual test call and a visible call record.
5.1.2 Account creation and first login
The app must support:
      * Sign up
      * Sign in
      * Sign out
      * Sign out of all devices
      * Account deletion with explicit confirmation
The first-time user flow must ensure the user reaches the main home screen successfully after authentication.
5.1.3 Device registration for notifications
On onboarding, the app must:
      * Request push notification permission
      * Confirm whether permission was granted
      * Offer a “Send test notification” action to validate setup
If notifications are denied:
      * The app must still function
      * The app must explain what becomes less immediate (for example, no instant call alerts)
      * The user must be able to re-enable from settings later
5.1.4 Biometric unlock enablement
On onboarding, the app must:
      * Explain what biometrics protect (sensitive call content)
      * Prompt the user to enable biometric gating
      * Support device-level fallback (PIN/passcode) if biometrics fail
Minimum protection scope:
      * Transcript
      * Recording playback (if recording is enabled)
      * Any sensitive call detail panels as defined by privacy policy
5.1.5 Notification privacy mode selection
On onboarding, the user must choose notification privacy behavior with a safe default:
      * Private mode (default): lock screen shows generic text only (example: “New call screened”)
      * Preview mode: lock screen may show caller name or number and a one-line reason snippet
Private mode must not show caller name, message content, transcript text, or reason snippet on the lock screen.
5.1.6 Call modes selection (setup decision)
On onboarding, the user must select which call modes to enable:
      * Dedicated AI number mode
      * Conditional call forwarding fallback mode
      * Both
Onboarding must explain in simple terms:
      * Dedicated AI number is a separate number callers dial directly
      * Forwarding mode routes calls to AI only when the user is busy or unreachable
5.1.7 Dedicated AI number provisioning flow
If dedicated number mode is enabled:
      * The system must provision a dedicated number for the user automatically
      * The app must display the number clearly with:
      * Copy button
      * Share action
      * The app must let the user set “Who can reach my AI number”:
      * Everyone (default)
      * Contacts only
      * VIP only
The onboarding must include a test call flow:
      * User triggers “Test my AI number”
      * User calls the AI number from another phone
      * App verifies:
      * AI answered
      * Call record exists
      * Summary exists or is shown as “processing”
      * Transcript exists or is shown as “processing”
      * Notification arrived (if enabled)
5.1.8 Conditional call forwarding setup flow (busy and unreachable)
Forwarding mode is required in Phase 1 (even if dedicated number is also used). The app must provide a guided setup with:
      * Explanation that forwarding depends on carrier and phone settings
      * Step-by-step instructions for enabling:
      * Forward on busy
      * Forward on unreachable
      * A dedicated “Test forwarding” tool with two required tests:
Test 1 (AI should NOT answer):
      * Call the user’s personal number while the user is available
      * Expected: AI does not answer
      * If AI answers, the app must warn about likely unconditional forwarding misconfiguration and provide fix steps
Test 2 (AI SHOULD answer):
      * Simulate busy or unreachable state
      * Call again
      * Expected: AI answers and creates a call record
      * App verifies summary and transcript availability and notification delivery
The app must include a troubleshooting checklist for common carrier behavior mismatches.
5.1.9 Minimal assistant configuration during onboarding
Before onboarding completes, the user must configure:
      * Assistant name
      * Voice preset
      * Temperament preset
      * Business hours
      * Quiet hours
      * Notification privacy mode confirmed
      * Text back enabled, with approval-first enforced
Optional onboarding steps:
      * Add at least one VIP
      * Run a second test call to confirm end-to-end reliability
5.1.10 Onboarding completion definition
Onboarding is complete only when the user sees:
      * A call record in call history
      * A readable summary (or “summary processing” state that completes successfully)
      * A transcript (or “transcript processing” state that completes successfully)
      * A notification event (or a clear explanation if notifications are disabled)
________________


5.2 Call handling behaviors
5.2.1 Core call objectives (Phase 1)
Default objective must be “screen and summarize”:
      * Capture structured details fast
      * Avoid long conversations
      * Produce reliable outputs for user action
Alternative objective must exist:
      * “Take a message only” (shorter, voicemail style)
5.2.2 Standard screening flow (minimum required steps)
The assistant must follow a consistent sequence:
      1. Greeting and identity
      * Introduce assistant name
      * State the user is unavailable
      * Ask permission to take a message
      2. Caller name capture
      * Ask for name
      * If refused, ask how the user should refer to them
      3. Reason for calling
      * Ask what the call is about
      * If vague, ask for a one-sentence summary
      4. Callback number confirmation
      * If caller number is visible, confirm last digits
      * If not visible, ask for a callback number and repeat for confirmation
      5. Urgency question
      * Ask if urgent or can wait
      * Map caller answer into: Low, Normal, Urgent
      6. Optional best callback time
      * Ask for best time to call back
      * For urgent calls, ask “by when do you need a call back”
      7. Close
      * Confirm the message will be passed along
      * End cleanly once required fields are captured
5.2.3 Required captured fields (structured)
For every AI-handled call, the system must store:
Required:
      * Call start time, end time, duration
      * Call source mode: dedicated AI number or forwarded from personal number
      * Caller phone number (if available)
      * Caller display name (as provided)
      * Reason for call (as captured)
      * Callback number (confirmed or provided)
      * Urgency label (Low, Normal, Urgent)
      * Transcript
      * Summary
      * Labels: VIP status, spam label
Optional:
      * Company or relationship (when asked for unknown callers)
      * Best callback time
      * Urgency reason note (why the system marked it urgent)
      * Spam reason note (why the system marked it possible spam)
      * Whether live handoff was offered, accepted, expired, failed
      * Recording metadata if enabled
5.2.4 Call duration limits (defaults)
Default maximum call length:
      * Standard callers: 3 minutes
      * VIP callers: 5 minutes
If required fields are captured earlier, the assistant must end earlier.
If time is running out:
      * The assistant must warn briefly and ask for the key message
      * Then close and store partial capture cleanly
5.2.5 Special flows
VIP caller flow:
      * More direct path to urgency
      * More accommodating tone
      * Live handoff can be triggered by rules if enabled
Unknown caller flow:
      * Capture standard fields
      * Ask optional “personal or calling on behalf of a company”
Sales or solicitation flow:
      * Capture one-sentence reason and contact details
      * End quickly
      * Apply “Sales” spam label (not auto-block)
Blocked caller flow:
      * Do not perform screening
      * End quickly with neutral behavior
      * Default: no call record and no notification for blocked attempts
      * Optional settings may allow logging blocked attempts
Distressed or emotional caller flow:
      * Calm tone
      * Focus on capturing the main message and urgency
      * Do not provide counseling or advice beyond basic guidance
5.2.6 Spam detection and labeling (conservative)
Spam labeling must be conservative and never auto-block by default.
Possible spam cues include:
      * Refusal to provide name and reason
      * Prerecorded audio patterns
      * Long silence or low-content repeats
      * Repeated short calls in a short window
Spam labels supported:
      * Possible spam
      * Sales
      * Unknown
      * Normal call
Users must be able to override and correct labels.
5.2.7 Urgency labeling rules
User-facing urgency labels:
      * Low
      * Normal
      * Urgent
Automatic urgency cues can include:
      * Caller explicitly says urgent or emergency
      * Safety or medical keywords
      * VIP rule boost if enabled
      * Repeat attempts in a short window
The system must store a short reason for auto-urgency when applied.
Users must be able to override urgency on the call detail screen.
5.2.8 Recording and consent behavior
Recording is off by default. If recording is enabled:
      * The assistant must announce recording at the start of the call
      * Recording metadata must be attached to the call record
      * Recording playback must be protected by biometric gating
If recording fails:
      * The call record must still exist
      * The UI must show “Recording unavailable” with a simple reason if known
5.2.9 Transcript disclosure behavior
The assistant must disclose transcription and summarization during the call (not only in legal text).
Disclosure must be short and consistent, aligned with privacy settings and policy.
5.2.10 Failure behavior when AI cannot answer
If the AI call handler fails or the AI provider is unavailable:
      * The call must not drop silently
      * The system must route to a safe fallback path, such as message-taking behavior or telephony-level fallback where possible
      * The user must receive an error notification that the call was not fully processed
Calls that fail mid-flow must still generate a minimal call record when possible (with partial transcript and “summary unavailable”).
________________


5.3 Call record output
5.3.1 Call record as the single source of truth
Every AI-handled call must produce exactly one call record (unless blocked attempts are configured to produce none). Call records must sync across all user devices.
5.3.2 Summary requirements
Summary must be:
      * Short and scannable
      * Consistent format
      * 3 to 6 bullets maximum
      * Never guess when uncertain
Minimum bullets should cover:
      * Who called (name and number if available)
      * Why they called (one sentence)
      * What they want the user to do
      * Urgency label and short reason
      * Best callback time if provided
If call ended early:
      * Summary must explicitly say screening was incomplete
If uncertain:
      * Summary must explicitly state uncertainty
5.3.3 Transcript requirements
Transcript must be:
      * Complete by speaker turns (Caller vs Assistant)
      * Stored as part of the call record
      * Protected by biometric gating (minimum requirement)
      * Usable for copy and share actions (share must warn about privacy)
5.3.4 Labels and reasons
Each call record must include:
      * Urgency label and optional reason
      * VIP status
      * Spam label and optional reason
      * Call source label: dedicated AI number or forwarded from personal number
Labels must be editable by the user. All changes must sync across devices.
5.3.5 Call history list requirements
Each call history row must show:
      * Caller display (name or number)
      * One-line summary snippet
      * Urgency badge
      * VIP badge if relevant
      * Spam badge if relevant
      * Timestamp
      * Source label (dedicated vs forwarded)
5.3.6 Call detail screen structure
Call detail must be ordered:
      1. Summary
      2. Key info panel
      3. Action buttons
      4. Transcript
      5. Recording (if enabled)
Key info panel must include:
      * Caller name and number
      * Callback number
      * Urgency label and reason
      * Call source
      * Call duration
      * Handoff offered and result (if applicable)
5.3.7 Durable creation and partial states
Call record creation must be durable even when post-processing fails.
Required failure states:
      * Transcript processing
      * Summary processing
      * Summary unavailable with retry option
      * Recording unavailable
      * Partial capture (caller hung up early)
The call record must still appear in call history with meaningful status text.
5.3.8 Retention and deletion linkages
When a call record is deleted (manual or retention expiry), the system must delete:
      * Summary
      * Transcript
      * Recording file and link if any
      * Notes
      * Reminder links tied to that call
      * Action history tied to that call
Deletion must be irreversible and must require confirmation for user-initiated deletes.
________________


5.4 Follow-up actions
5.4.1 Action set required in Phase 1
From call detail, the user must be able to:
      * Call back
      * Text back (approval-first)
      * Add note
      * Set reminder
      * Mark spam or correct label
      * Block or unblock number
      * Add to VIP or remove from VIP (where applicable)
Each action must be stored on the call record as activity history and must sync across devices.
5.4.2 Call back behavior
      * One-tap call back using the device’s normal dialer
      * If callback number is missing, disable the action with clear text
      * If caller number differs from provided callback number, show both and let the user choose
Store:
      * Callback initiated timestamp
5.4.3 Text back behavior (approval-first enforced)
Texting must be approval-first for all users and all callers by default.
Flow:
      1. User taps “Text back”
      2. App displays AI-generated draft (auto-created after call)
      3. User edits or approves
      4. User taps Send
      5. System sends via messaging provider
      6. Call record stores:
      * Draft content
      * Final content
      * Timestamps
      * Delivery status
      * Failure reason if applicable
If sending fails:
      * Show clear status
      * Allow retry
      * Preserve draft
Attachments are not required in Phase 1.
5.4.4 Notes
Notes must:
      * Be editable
      * Be separate from transcript
      * Sync across devices
      * Be treated as sensitive content if biometric gating is enabled
5.4.5 Reminders
Reminders must be:
      * One-time reminders (recurring reminders are not required in Phase 1)
      * Linked to a call record
      * Delivered via push notifications
      * Visible in a reminder list screen:
      * Upcoming
      * Completed
      * Overdue
If notifications are disabled:
      * Reminders must still exist
      * The app must warn that reminders will not alert
If a call record is deleted:
      * Linked reminders must be deleted by default to prevent orphaned items
5.4.6 Templates and suggested drafts
Phase 1 must include an initial set of text templates (minimum target: 10 common situations), such as:
      * Busy now, share key details by text
      * Will call back today, what time works best
      * Please send one message with the details
      * Thanks, received your message, will follow up
Template UI must allow:
      * Selecting a template
      * Editing before sending
      * Optional saving of user custom templates can exist but must remain simple if included
After each call, the app may show suggested actions:
      * Suggested reply draft
      * Set reminder
      * Block number (only when spam cues exist)
Nothing may be auto-sent without approval.
________________


5.5 Live handoff flow
5.5.1 Live handoff feature definition
Live handoff allows the user to take over an active call after the AI answers, based on user rules.
5.5.2 User controls and trigger rules
The app must provide:
      * Live handoff toggle: On or Off
      * Rule selection when On:
      * VIP only
      * Urgent only
      * VIP and Urgent
5.5.3 Minimum screening before prompting takeover
Before offering handoff, the AI must attempt to capture at least:
