      * Caller identifier (name or usable identifier)
      * One-line reason
For highly urgent calls, the system may prompt earlier, but must still aim to provide context.
5.5.4 Prompt delivery and device behavior
When eligible:
      * Send push notification (if enabled)
      * Send in-app real-time event if the app is open
      * All devices receive the prompt
      * Only one device may successfully accept
      * If multiple accept attempts occur, first valid acceptance wins
Prompt content must respect notification privacy mode:
      * Private mode: “Call takeover available” only
      * Preview mode: caller name or number, one-line reason, urgency badge
5.5.5 Timeout and ignore behavior
      * Handoff offer must expire after a short window (default target: 15 to 25 seconds)
      * If ignored or expired, AI continues screening and ends normally
      * Call record must show:
      * Handoff offered: Yes
      * Handoff result: ignored, expired, failed, or success
5.5.6 Takeover connection behavior
When the user taps “Take call now”:
      * App shows connecting state immediately
      * Backend verifies:
      * Call is still active
      * Offer has not expired
      * User session and device are valid
      * Call belongs to that user
On success:
      * AI stops speaking and exits the call path cleanly
      * Call record marks handoff success with timestamps and accepting device
On failure:
      * AI resumes screening immediately so caller is not left in silence
      * User receives clear failure feedback
      * Call record marks handoff attempted and failed with reason if known
5.5.7 Call forwarding loop prevention (critical)
Because forwarding is enabled on the user’s personal number, a takeover attempt that calls the user’s personal number can be forwarded back to the AI number if the user remains busy or unreachable. This can create an unintended loop or false answer.
Phase 1 must include loop prevention rules:
      * The takeover attempt must be time-limited and single-attempt by default
      * If the takeover leg is forwarded back to the AI number:
      * The AI number handler must detect it as a forwarded takeover leg
      * The system must refuse or immediately end that leg
      * The system must mark takeover as failed due to “user unreachable”
      * The AI must continue screening the original caller without interruption
Additionally, takeover dialing must use voicemail detection:
      * If the user’s voicemail answers the takeover leg, treat as failure and resume AI screening
      * Never bridge the caller into the user’s voicemail as a “successful takeover”
5.5.8 Required stored handoff fields
Call record must store:
      * handoff_offered_at
      * handoff_offer_reason (VIP, urgent, both)
      * handoff_accepted_at (if accepted)
      * handoff_accepted_device_id (if accepted)
      * handoff_result (success, failed, expired, ignored)
      * handoff_failure_reason (if failed)
________________


5.6 Settings and personalization
5.6.1 Settings categories (Phase 1)
Settings must be grouped clearly:
      1. Account
      2. Devices
      3. Assistant identity and voice
      4. Temperament and language style
      5. Call handling and call objectives
      6. Availability (business hours) and quiet hours
      7. VIP, block, spam controls
      8. Texting rules and templates
      9. Privacy and security controls
      10. Memory and retention
All settings must sync across devices with predictable conflict behavior:
      * Last write wins is acceptable if applied consistently and visible.
5.6.2 Assistant identity and voice
User must be able to set:
      * Assistant name (used in greeting)
      * Voice preset selection with preview
      * Consistency requirement: selected voice must be stable across calls and devices
5.6.3 Temperament presets
User must be able to pick a preset that controls:
      * Formal vs casual style
      * Humor level
      * Directness
      * Swearing rules (default: no swearing)
5.6.4 Call handling settings
User must be able to configure:
      * Call objective: screen and summarize (default) or take message only
      * Max call length options:
      * Standard callers: 2, 3 (default), 5 minutes
      * VIP callers: 3, 5 (default) minutes
      * Dedicated AI number access policy:
      * Everyone
      * Contacts only
      * VIP only
      * Recording toggle (default off)
5.6.5 Business hours
User must be able to set:
      * Days of week
      * Start time and end time
      * After-hours behavior:
      * Screen normally
      * Take message only
      * Screen and mark low priority by default (VIP and urgent can override)
5.6.6 Quiet hours
User must be able to set:
      * Days of week
      * Start time and end time
      * Quiet hours behavior:
      * Silent for normal calls
      * Normal alert for Important calls only (recommended default)
5.6.7 VIP list
Must support:
      * Add manually (number and optional label)
      * Add from call detail
      * Remove VIP
      * VIP badge and filters
      * VIP impacts:
      * Mark Important by default
      * Longer call duration defaults
      * Eligible for live handoff depending on rules
5.6.8 Block list
Must support:
      * Block from call detail (with confirmation)
      * Block manually
      * Unblock
      * Default blocked handling:
      * End quickly
      * No call record and no notification by default
Optional advanced toggles may exist:
         * Log blocked attempts
         * Notify blocked attempts
5.6.9 Memory (enabled by default) and memory page
Memory must be light and preference-based only.
Requirements:
         * Memory toggle on or off
         * Memory page where users can:
         * View memory items
         * Search memory items
         * Delete individual items
         * Delete all memory
         * Memory creation must be conservative:
         * Implicit memory allowed only for safe categories (callback time preference, name correction, summary length preference, template usage)
         * Any broader memory must require explicit user action
If memory is turned off:
         * No new memory is created
         * User must be offered:
         * Turn off and keep existing memory
         * Turn off and delete all memory now
5.6.10 Retention and deletion controls
User must be able to configure retention with simple options:
         * 7 days
         * 30 days
         * 90 days
Retention must apply consistently to:
         * Summaries
         * Transcripts
         * Recordings (if enabled)
         * Notes
         * Action history
         * Call-linked reminders
         * Call-linked memory references
User must be able to:
         * Delete a single call record (confirmed)
         * Delete all call data (strong confirmation)
         * Delete account (explicit confirmation)
Local caching rules:
         * Offline use is not a Phase 1 requirement
         * Any cached sensitive content on device must be encrypted and cleared on logout, device revoke, delete all, or account delete
________________


5.7 Admin portal functions (internal support and operations)
5.7.1 Portal audience and access
The portal is for operational use by the delivery organization (your team plus Matt’s team). It is not an end-user web dashboard.
Portal must support role-based access at minimum:
         * Admin
         * Support
         * Viewer
5.7.2 Core portal capabilities required
The portal must allow authorized operators to:
User management:
         * View user list and account status
         * View assigned dedicated AI number per user
         * View device list per user (high level identifiers and last active)
         * Force sign-out all devices (admin action)
         * Disable an account (support action)
         * Trigger account deletion workflows only through approved process (not one-click destructive by default)
Call operations:
         * Search and view call records by user, date, caller number, labels
         * View call processing status (transcript or summary pending, failed)
         * Retry summary generation (if supported by backend)
         * Inspect handoff outcomes (offered, accepted, failed) and reasons
Abuse and cost control support:
         * View spam-heavy numbers hitting the platform
         * Apply platform-level rate limiting rules where available
         * Flag a user account for investigation (support workflow)
Configuration support:
         * Manage global template sets shipped in the app (read-only is acceptable if write access is risky)
         * Manage feature flags if feature flags are used (recommended for risky features like recording and handoff)
Audit and evidence:
         * View audit logs for security-relevant events (sign-in anomalies, device revokes, deletion actions, handoff actions)
         * Export logs for internal debugging (not end-user export)
5.7.3 Explicit portal non-goals for Phase 1
The portal must not include:
         * End-user access
         * CRM sync controls
         * Outbound campaign tools
         * Appointment booking tools
         * Email inbox tools
         * WhatsApp tools
5.7.4 Safety requirements for portal actions
Any risky action must require confirmation and be logged:
         * Blocking or disabling an account
         * Force sign-out all devices
         * Retention policy changes if exposed
         * Deletion actions
All portal actions must be scoped and attributable to an operator identity via audit logs.
________________


CHAPTER 5: FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS CATALOG
5.0 Purpose and scope of this chapter
This chapter is the master checklist of Phase 1 behaviors and user-visible outcomes. It defines what the product must do, how users experience it, and the expected system outcomes for each capability. It is written to be testable: every requirement should map to at least one QA test case and at least one acceptance criterion.
Phase 1 is a call screening concierge workflow with post-call summaries, transcripts, and simple follow-up actions. Users control settings such as voice, call handling rules, live handoff, and retention. Security and privacy controls are treated as mandatory MVP scope.
________________


5.1 Onboarding and setup flows
5.1.1 Onboarding entry and completion criteria
Goal: a user installs the app, signs in, completes required setup, and can verify end-to-end that inbound calls are handled and produce call records.
Onboarding is complete when:
         1. The user can sign in and remain signed in across devices.
         2. A device record exists in backend and push notifications can be delivered.
         3. The user has a dedicated AI number provisioned and visible in-app.
         4. Forwarding mode is configured and verified (Phase 1 requirement).
         5. Live handoff is configured (Phase 1 scope includes live handoff).
         6. Privacy defaults are applied and biometric gating is configured.
         7. A test call can be performed that results in a call record with summary and transcript states.
5.1.2 Account creation, identity uniqueness, and lifecycle actions
Behavior requirements:
         * Each user account must have a unique identity.
         * A user can log in consistently across devices.
         * A user can recover access if they lose a device.
         * A user can revoke devices and sessions.
         * Phase 1 must support: sign up, sign in, sign out, sign out of all devices, password/login method change if applicable, and account deletion (with explicit confirmation).
5.1.3 Device registration, push enablement, and multi-device baseline
Device definition (Phase 1): any signed-in mobile device eligible to receive notifications, such as primary phone, secondary phone, tablet. Each device must have a backend record.
Multi-device expectations:
         * Call history and call detail are identical across devices after sync.
         * Settings changes propagate across devices after synchronization.
         * Notifications go to all devices unless disabled per device.
         * Revoking a device removes access immediately.
Device management UI requirements:
         * Show device name/label, last active time, notification status, and a remove option.
5.1.4 Permissions and privacy-first onboarding prompts
Onboarding must request and explain permissions only when needed, with clear user benefit.
Minimum expected permissions in Phase 1:
         * Notifications permission (required to support call screened alerts, handoff offers, reminders).
         * Contacts permission (only if “Contacts only” access control is enabled or if user opts into contacts-based features).
Privacy defaults must be applied immediately:
         * Notifications are private by default.
         * Lock screen shows only “New call screened” and does not show caller name or message content.
         * User must open the app and unlock to see details.
5.1.5 Dedicated AI number provisioning flow (required)
Goal: each user receives a dedicated AI phone number (Twilio-managed) that callers dial to reach the assistant.
Provisioning requirements:
         * Number is auto-provisioned at onboarding.
         * The user can view and copy the AI number.
         * The system clearly explains that callers must dial the AI number unless forwarding mode is set up.
         * Provisioning failures must be surfaced with actionable retry steps (for example: “Try again”, “Contact support”, “Choose different area code” if later supported).
5.1.6 Forwarding setup guidance and verification (required)
Forwarding mode is required in Phase 1.
Forwarding goal: when a caller dials the user’s real number and the user is busy/unreachable, the call forwards to the dedicated AI number for screening.
Setup guidance requirements:
         * Provide device-specific instructions for iOS and Android.
         * Explain carrier dependency and that conditional forwarding behavior varies.
         * Provide a built-in “Forwarding Test” wizard that guides the user through validation and captures which trigger(s) work on their carrier. The result becomes part of “known behavior” and is reflected in the in-app guidance to reduce surprises.
Supported forwarding triggers (Phase 1):
         * Busy
         * Unreachable
         * The app must guide users to configure both, because the user explicitly requires forwarding and wants the best-supported option. (Carrier limitations must be documented in-app.)
Verification behavior:
         * The test wizard triggers controlled scenarios (or instructs the user) and confirms whether the forwarded call reaches the AI and produces a call record.
         * If forwarding fails, the wizard must capture the failure type (carrier limitation, misconfiguration, device state) and provide next steps (for example: “Enable unconditional forwarding as a fallback” if allowed by product policy, or “Use AI number directly”).
5.1.7 Live handoff setup (Phase 1 scope)
Onboarding must allow the user to enable live handoff and choose their handoff behavior (rules are defined in section 5.5). Live handoff can be toggled by the user in settings.
5.1.8 Biometrics gating prompts (required)
Biometrics protect sensitive call content. Minimum protected items:
         * Transcripts
         * Recording playback if recordings are enabled
         * Detailed caller information and notes when treated as sensitive by product rules
Biometric unlock should be required:
         * When opening call detail content the first time after app launch
         * After inactivity window
         * When opening recording playback
         * When viewing or exporting sensitive data (export is not in Phase 1, but the gate should be designed to extend later)
Fallback behavior:
         * If biometrics fail, fall back to device-level secure fallback method.
________________


5.2 Call handling behaviors
5.2.1 Primary call modes in Phase 1
Phase 1 supports a call concierge workflow where the AI answers inbound calls and screens them. The product must support:
         * Dedicated AI number inbound flow (callers dial AI number directly).
         * Forwarded call flow (calls forwarded from the user’s real number to the AI number on busy/unreachable).
         * Live handoff during screening (see section 5.5).
5.2.2 Screening objectives and conversation policy (default)
Screening objective: collect the information needed for the user to decide what to do next. The flow must feel natural and human-friendly with light small talk only when it helps clarity, but must remain efficient.
Must capture (Phase 1):
         * Who is calling
         * Why they are calling
         * How urgent it is
         * How to reach them back
If caller refuses questions:
         * Fall back to message-taking so the call still produces something useful.
Assistant identity and call behavior rules:
         * Be polite and efficient.
         * Collect required information every time.
         * Avoid long conversations and end cleanly once the goal is achieved.
         * Never pretend to be the user. The AI is presented as an assistant.
         * Support user control through settings, including call length and live handoff.
5.2.3 Standard call flow steps (default behavior)
The default screening sequence must implement:
         1. Greeting and identity: assistant introduction, state user is unavailable, ask permission to take a message.
         2. Ask for caller name with fallback if refused.
         3. Ask reason for calling with gentle prompt for clarity if vague.
         4. Confirm callback number (confirm visible caller ID or request number if hidden).
         5. Ask urgency question (Low, Normal, Urgent interpreted from natural language).
         6. Optional best time to call back (stronger version if urgent).
         7. Close politely and end the call once the goal is achieved.
5.2.4 Required captured fields and minimum extraction
For every screened call, the system must store at minimum:
         * Caller phone number (as observed by telephony provider when available)
         * Caller name (raw text, may be partial)
         * Reason for call (short summary text, plus transcript context)
         * Urgency level (Low, Normal, Urgent)
         * Best callback time (optional)
         * Callback number confirmation result (confirmed, provided, unknown)
         * Call timestamps (start, answered, ended)
         * Call mode metadata (dedicated vs forwarded vs handoff occurred)
5.2.5 Call length limits and termination behavior
Default call length limits in Phase 1:
         * Standard callers: max 3 minutes
         * VIP callers: max 5 minutes
If required fields are collected early, the AI ends sooner. If time limit is reached, the AI must end cleanly while preserving collected information and generating a call record.
5.2.6 Special handling rules: VIP, blocked, suspected spam, unknown
The system must support user-controlled lists and simple classification that affect call handling and notification importance.
Minimum list types for Phase 1:
         * VIP allow list
         * Block list
Minimum behaviors:
         * VIP calls are visually flagged (VIP badge) and treated as Important for notifications where configured.
         * Blocked numbers follow blocked handling rules and do not expose sensitive content. Blocking must be reversible by user.
Spam handling posture in Phase 1 should be conservative:
         * Provide spam labeling and block marking that is reversible.
         * If advanced auto-block is included, it must be gated and verifiable.
5.2.7 Voicemail detection (required)
Voicemail detection is required in Phase 1. The system must detect voicemail-like answers and avoid long screening conversations with voicemail systems where possible, while still producing a useful call record.
5.2.8 Recording behavior and disclosures (Phase 1 rules)
Recording in Phase 1:
         * Off by default
         * User can enable it
         * If enabled, AI announces recording at the start
         * Recording playback is protected by biometrics
Transcript and summarization disclosure:
         * Calls are transcribed and summarized, and disclosure is required (implementation details are defined in later chapters, but Phase 1 behavior must include disclosure either in-call or via legal text, per final compliance design).
________________


5.3 Call record output
5.3.1 Call record contents (minimum)
After each call, the user receives a call record containing:
         * Concise summary written for fast reading
         * Full transcript for detail
         * Labels such as urgency and possible spam
         * Optional recording link if enabled by the user
5.3.2 Summary format and quality requirements
Minimum summary expectations:
         * 3 to 6 bullets
         * Must include reason and callback details
         * Must handle uncertainty by stating uncertainty, not guessing
         * If summary fails, call record still exists and clearly shows missing summary state
5.3.3 Transcript requirements
Transcript requirements:
         * Speaker separation
         * Readable formatting for quick scanning
         * Must be tied to the call record and synced across devices
5.3.4 Partial records and processing states
The system must support processing states at minimum:
         * Transcript pending, ready, failed
         * Summary pending, ready, failed
         * Recording pending, ready, failed (if recording enabled)
If any post-processing fails:
         * The call record remains visible
         * The UI shows the missing component state and offers a retry when appropriate (as defined later in queue/retry chapters)
5.3.5 Multi-device synchronization expectations
Call record creation and updates must synchronize across devices:
         * Call list updates appear on all signed-in devices
         * Call detail updates (summary ready, transcript ready, actions taken) appear consistently after sync
5.3.6 Privacy surfaces for call records
Call list and notification surfaces must respect privacy:
         * Private by default notifications show only generic “New call screened” on lock screen.
         * Call list may show safe metadata without unlocking (configurable by privacy policy), but sensitive content like transcript must require biometric unlock.
________________


5.4 Follow-up actions
5.4.1 Action set from a call record (Phase 1)
From the call record, the user must be able to:
         * Call back
         * Text back with approval-first behavior
         * Add a note
         * Set a reminder
         * Block a number
5.4.2 Call back action
Requirements:
         * Tap-to-call back uses the confirmed callback number when available.
         * Action is logged in the call record activity timeline (for user traceability and support debugging).
5.4.3 Text back action (approval-first always)
Texting in Phase 1:
         * AI can draft a suggested message (based on call context) but cannot send it automatically.
         * User must explicitly approve before any message is sent.
         * Draft must be editable by the user.
         * Template selection should be supported if included in Phase 1 templates list.
Failure behavior:
         * If sending fails, preserve the draft and show a retry option and failure reason category (network, provider, rate limit, invalid number). The action state must remain consistent across devices after sync.
5.4.4 Notes
Requirements:
         * User can add a note to a call record.
         * Notes must save reliably and sync across devices.
5.4.5 Reminders (required) and reminder notification behavior
Requirements:
         * User can set at least one-time reminders from a call record.
         * Reminder triggers a notification at the correct time.
         * Reminders are visible in a reminders list and linked back to the call record.
         * Reminder notifications respect quiet hours rules as configured (normal reminders may be quieted, Important reminders may still fire based on settings).
5.4.6 Block and spam marking
Requirements:
         * User can block a number from the call record.
         * User can mark as spam and undo.
         * Blocked handling rules must be enforced consistently in future calls from that number.
________________


5.5 Live handoff flow (Phase 1 scope)
5.5.1 Live handoff purpose and constraints
Live handoff allows the user to “take over” a call while the AI is screening, based on user-configured eligibility rules. It exists to handle high-priority calls without forcing the user to answer unknown calls blindly.
5.5.2 Eligibility rules and trigger timing
Phase 1 must support eligibility rules that can be controlled by settings, with a safe default (defined later in the handoff chapter). At minimum, eligibility must consider:
         * VIP status
         * Urgency status (as captured by the AI screening question)
Trigger timing requirements:
         * Handoff prompt must arrive fast enough to be useful.
         * Handoff triggers only for eligible calls.
         * If the user ignores or declines, AI continues screening and produces a normal call record.
5.5.3 Offer, notification, and multi-device arbitration
Requirements:
         * When a handoff is offered, all eligible devices may receive the prompt, but only one device can accept.
         * Acceptance must be atomic: first accept wins, all others see accepted/expired state.
Privacy requirement:
         * In private mode, lock screen must not show caller details even for handoff prompts.
5.5.4 Handoff destination in Phase 1
Phase 1 handoff destination is the user’s real phone number (PSTN), not an in-app VoIP call. This supports the simplest, most reliable behavior given Phase 1 scope and avoids building a full in-app calling stack. (Technical implementation is defined in the handoff integration chapter.)
5.5.5 Timeout, decline, and failure behavior
Requirements:
         * Handoff offer has a timeout window. If timeout occurs, AI continues screening without breaking the call.
         * Decline behaves the same as ignore: AI continues screening.
         * If connecting to the user fails (busy, unreachable, no-answer), the system must not loop indefinitely.
5.5.6 Loop prevention rules (required)
To prevent loops when handoff destination is the user’s phone number:
         * The system must enforce a single handoff attempt per call.
         * If the user does not successfully connect, the AI must continue screening and then end the call cleanly based on normal flow, rather than re-attempting handoff.
         * The system must apply suppression rules if it detects the forwarded call is re-entering the AI number again due to carrier forwarding settings. (Exact detection signals and suppression design are defined in the handoff technical design chapter.)
5.5.7 Handoff event recording in call record
The call record must store handoff timeline events:
         * Offered timestamp
         * Accepted timestamp and accepting device
         * Connect attempt result (connected, failed, timed out, declined)
This is required for QA verification and support debugging.
________________


5.6 Settings and personalization
5.6.1 Settings must exist in Phase 1
Phase 1 includes a customer-facing settings area that serves as the personalization dashboard. Users can control:
            * Voice selection and tone preset
            * Business hours behavior
            * VIP allow list and block list
            * Whether live handoff is enabled and under what conditions
            * Whether outbound texts require approval (default yes)
            * Whether call recording is enabled (default off)
            * Data retention duration and deletion controls
            * Memory on/off and memory deletion controls
5.6.2 Notification preferences, privacy mode, and quiet hours
Requirements:
            * Notification privacy mode exists and defaults to private.
            * Private mode lock screen content is generic (“New call screened”) with no caller name and no message content.
            * Quiet hours are required in Phase 1.
            * Notification reliability must include retry and delivery tracking.
            * Multi-device notifications must arrive and deep link to the correct call record.
5.6.3 Memory page and deletion controls (required)
Memory rules:
            * Memory is on by default.
            * Memory can store caller names and preferences.
            * Memory must be visible and deletable in-app.
            * Deletion controls must be user-accessible and must take effect across devices.
5.6.4 Retention and deletion controls (required)
Requirements:
            * User can delete a single call record.
            * User can delete all call data (behavior defined in later retention chapter).
            * Retention duration is configurable and must be implemented consistently for all included object types (final policy values and scope are locked in retention chapter).
Account deletion:
            * Must be explicit and confirmed because it affects call records and memory. The deletion model is finalized in later chapters, but Phase 1 must provide the user-facing capability.
________________


5.7 Admin portal functions (internal operator portal)
5.7.1 Portal exists in Phase 1 and is internal-only
Phase 1 includes a web admin portal for the team operating the app (not a consumer-facing web dashboard). An internal admin role exists for portal access.
5.7.2 Minimum portal capabilities
Minimum portal functions must support operations and support workflows without violating privacy expectations:
            1. User lookup
            * Find user by email, user ID, or AI phone number.
            * View account status (active, disabled), device list, last active times.
            2. Call logs and processing status
            * View call records at metadata level (timestamps, statuses, processing state).
            * View failure reasons for transcripts, summaries, notifications, and handoff attempts.
            3. Forwarding diagnostics support
            * Store and view forwarding test results per user (carrier category, trigger success).
            * Provide guidance templates for common carrier limitations.
            4. Deletion tools
            * Admin-assisted data deletion flows must exist for support escalation scenarios, with safeguards, confirmations, and audit logging.
            5. Role management
            * RBAC roles: Admin, Support, Viewer (exact permissions defined in authorization chapter).
5.7.3 Portal limitations (privacy and least privilege)
The portal must enforce least privilege and prevent cross-user leakage:
            * No data is shared between users by default.
            * Access is strictly scoped per user when supporting an issue.
            * Sensitive content (transcripts, recordings) must only be accessible under explicitly designed support policies and must be logged as sensitive access.
________________


5.8 Chapter 5 completion checklist
This chapter is complete when:
            * Every Phase 1 user-visible capability is listed with required behaviors and failure states.
            * Every requirement is testable and maps to at least one QA test case.
            * No Phase 2+ features are implicitly included (only Phase 1 scope and controls).
________________


Chapter 6: Non-Functional Requirements
6.0 Purpose and scope of this chapter
This chapter defines the non-functional requirements for Phase 1. These are the constraints and quality targets that apply to every feature, including call handling, summaries, texting, live handoff, notifications, retention, deletion, and the internal admin portal.
Non-functional requirements answer questions like:
            * How secure must it be?
            * How private must it be by default?
            * How reliable must it be under failure?
            * How fast must it feel during a call?
            * How much load must it handle without redesign?
            * How do we keep it maintainable with a small team and a two-month deadline?
These requirements are mandatory for Phase 1 because the system processes personal communications and can store sensitive content (transcripts, summaries, optional recordings).
________________


6.1 Security requirements
6.1.1 Security goals for Phase 1
Phase 1 must achieve these outcomes:
            1. Only the correct user can access their data (calls, transcripts, summaries, recordings, lists, settings).
            2. Cross-user data exposure is prevented by design and verified by tests.
            3. Sensitive content is protected at rest and in transit.
            4. Device revocation removes access quickly and prevents old cached content from being readable.
            5. Public endpoints (including telephony webhooks) are protected against abuse and spoofing.
            6. Logs, monitoring, and admin tooling do not create privacy leaks.
            7. Operators can detect incidents quickly and take safe mitigation actions without redeploying.
6.1.2 Identity, authentication, and MFA
Required login methods in Phase 1
            * Email and password
            * Google login
MFA requirement
            * MFA is required for every account.
            * Allowed MFA methods:
            * TOTP authenticator apps (recommended default)
            * Email OTP (allowed but treated as weaker)
MFA enforcement rules
            * MFA is required before the user can access any sensitive content.
            * MFA is required again after high-risk events:
            * login from a new device
            * password reset
            * “sign out all devices”
            * changes to privacy-critical settings (recording, retention, notification privacy mode)
6.1.3 Session security and token rules
Session model requirements
            * Short-lived access token and long-lived refresh token pattern.
            * Access tokens expire quickly (recommended: 15 minutes).
            * Refresh tokens rotate and can be revoked per device.
Device binding
            * Each refresh token must be associated with a device record.
            * A stolen refresh token must be revocable by revoking that device.
Session revocation
            * User can sign out on the current device.
            * User can sign out all devices.
            * Admin portal can revoke sessions only under strict role permissions and audit logging.
Idle and maximum session age
            * Idle timeout: recommended 14 days (forced re-authentication after inactivity).
            * Maximum session age: recommended 90 days (forced re-authentication even if active).
            * If MFA method changes or password changes, revoke all refresh tokens.
6.1.4 Authorization and cross-user isolation
Backend enforcement
            * Authorization must be enforced server-side for every read and write.
            * Every query that reads user data must include user_id scoping as a hard requirement.
            * Never trust client-provided identifiers alone (call_id, recording_id, message_id).
Direct object access prevention
            * If a user guesses another user’s call_id, the backend must return not found (or forbidden) without leaking existence.
Admin portal isolation
            * Admin portal users are not the same as consumer users.
            * Admin portal access must use a separate auth boundary and separate roles.
            * Portal must enforce least privilege and must not allow bulk export of sensitive content.
6.1.5 Device security and revocation
Device revoke is a security feature, not a convenience feature.
When a device is revoked:
            * All tokens tied to that device become invalid immediately.
            * Realtime connections (websockets) for that device are terminated.
            * The app must be forced to re-authenticate on that device.
Client-side cache clearing
If the app caches any sensitive content (call summaries, transcripts, recordings metadata):
            * Cached data must be stored encrypted using platform secure storage mechanisms.
            * Cached data must be cleared on:
            * logout
            * device revoke
            * delete-all-call-data
            * account deletion
            * Even if cache clearing fails, sensitive screens must remain protected by biometric gating and server-side authorization must still prevent fetch.
6.1.6 Encryption in transit
Mandatory rule
            * All client to backend traffic must use TLS (HTTPS).
            * All admin portal traffic must use TLS (HTTPS).
            * All webhook endpoints must use HTTPS only.
Operational requirement
            * Force HTTPS redirects where applicable.
            * Disable weak TLS settings and enforce modern cipher suites.
            * Use HSTS for web surfaces (admin portal) to reduce downgrade risk.
6.1.7 Encryption at rest
Server-side encryption requirements
            * Database storage encryption must be enabled.
            * Object storage encryption must be enabled for recordings and any stored artifacts.
            * VM disk encryption must be enabled for volumes that may contain logs, temp files, or cached artifacts.
Scope of data considered sensitive at rest
            * Transcripts
            * Summaries
            * Optional recordings
            * Caller phone numbers and caller-provided identity fields
            * VIP list and block list
            * User settings that reveal behavior patterns (quiet hours, business hours)
            * Audit logs that include sensitive metadata
6.1.8 Secrets handling and key hygiene
Phase 1 must not embed secrets in mobile builds.
Allowed secret storage patterns
            * Local development:
            * .env files are allowed only on developer machines
            * .env must be gitignored
            * .env must never be copied into commits, screenshots, or tickets
            * CI/CD:
            * GitHub Secrets are used for build and deploy credentials
            * Production (free alternative to paid secrets managers):
            * AWS SSM Parameter Store (standard parameters) as the primary store for secrets and configuration
            * Instance role permissions must limit access to only needed parameters
            * Secrets are fetched at deploy or boot and injected as environment variables into containers
            * Do not write secrets into container images
Rotation requirement
            * Telephony API keys, AI provider keys, and signing secrets must be rotatable without code changes.
            * Rotation must be documented and testable.
6.1.9 Webhook security and request validation
Telephony and provider webhooks are high-risk public endpoints.
Webhook validation requirements
            * Verify provider signature headers for inbound webhooks (reject invalid signatures).
            * Enforce strict payload validation (schema validation).
            * Apply replay protection where feasible (timestamp tolerance and nonce caching where applicable).
            * Rate limit webhook endpoints to reduce abuse.
Input validation requirements
            * All API endpoints must validate:
            * types
            * required fields
            * maximum lengths
            * allowed enums
            * Normalize phone numbers to E.164 format on write.
            * Reject invalid phone numbers early.
6.1.10 Abuse prevention and rate limiting
Because inbound calls can be attacked and costs can spike, Phase 1 must include abuse controls.
Minimum rate limiting dimensions
            * Per caller number (to protect a user’s AI number)
            * Per user account (to prevent a single user from generating runaway costs)
            * Per IP (for app and admin portal APIs)
            * Per endpoint class (login, token refresh, sensitive reads)
Flood detection
            * Detect repeated calls from the same caller number within a short window.
            * Detect repeated calls across multiple users from the same caller number.
            * Provide automated mitigation paths:
            * temporary throttling
            * marking as suspected spam
            * optional temporary block at platform level (admin only, audited)
6.1.11 Audit logging security rules
Audit logs are required but must not become a data leak.
Audit events that must be recorded
            * Login and logout
            * MFA enrollment and changes
            * Device added and device revoked
            * Privacy-critical setting changes:
            * notification privacy mode
            * recording toggle
            * retention changes
            * memory toggle
            * live handoff rules
            * Data deletion:
            * delete call record
            * delete-all-call-data
            * delete account
            * Outbound actions:
            * text sent (approval and send result)
            * Live handoff events:
            * offered, accepted, failed, expired
Audit log content restrictions
            * Do not store full transcripts or summaries inside audit logs.
            * Do not store recording URLs without access control context.
            * Store only the minimum metadata needed to investigate (event type, timestamp, actor_id, device_id, object_id references, result status).
6.1.12 Secure development and dependency hygiene
Code-level requirements
            * No secrets in code or config committed to source control.
            * Use dependency scanning and update process.
            * Use static analysis and linting in CI for mobile, backend, and admin portal.
Access requirements
            * No shared production credentials.
            * Production access must be per-person and role-based.
            * SSH access (if required) must use key-based auth, minimal users, and restricted security group rules.
________________


6.2 Privacy requirements
6.2.1 Privacy goals for Phase 1
Phase 1 must meet these outcomes:
            1. Private content is not exposed accidentally on lock screens, notifications, logs, or admin tooling.
            2. Users understand that calls are transcribed and summarized.
            3. Users can control, view, and delete their stored data.
            4. Retention is enforced automatically and failures are visible to operators.
            5. Memory is user-visible and deletable.
6.2.2 Notification privacy (private by default)
Default behavior
            * Notifications are private by default.
            * Lock screen content must show generic text only:
            * “New call screened”
            * “Important call screened”
            * No caller name, no caller number, no message content, no reason snippet on lock screen by default.
Preview mode (optional)
            * Users may opt into preview mode.
            * Preview mode may show caller name or number and a one-line reason snippet.
            * Preview mode must be clearly labeled as less private.
6.2.3 Biometric gating for sensitive content
Minimum protected content
            * Transcript
            * Summary details beyond the minimal list view
            * Recording playback (if enabled)
            * Caller identity details beyond what is permitted by privacy mode
            * Notes and memory entries
Lock timing
            * Default must favor privacy with minimal annoyance.
            * Recommended default:
            * lock after 2 minutes of inactivity
            * lock immediately on app background if device is not trusted (future extension)
            * Biometric failure must fall back to device secure fallback. No bypass.
6.2.4 Data minimization rules
Phase 1 should store only what it needs to deliver the product promise.
Principles
            * Do not collect fields that are not used in Phase 1 UI or workflows.
            * Avoid storing raw provider payloads if structured fields suffice.
            * Store derived artifacts (summary, labels) but tie them to retention and deletion rules.
6.2.5 Disclosure and consent behaviors
Transcription and summarization disclosure
            * The assistant must disclose that the call is transcribed and summarized.
            * Disclosure must occur during the call (short, consistent sentence).
Recording consent
            * Recording is off by default.
            * If recording is enabled:
            * the assistant announces recording at the start of the call
            * the UI clearly indicates recording is enabled
6.2.6 Retention, deletion, and verified removal
Retention enforcement
            * Retention must run automatically. Users should not need to manually clean up.
            * Retention must apply to:
            * call records
            * transcripts
            * summaries
            * recordings (if enabled)
            * notes
            * call-linked reminders
            * action history
Verified deletion
When a user deletes a call record:
            * Server-side call record and all derived artifacts are deleted.
            * Storage objects (recordings) are deleted.
            * Device caches are cleared on next sync and become inaccessible immediately through gating and authorization.
When a user triggers delete-all-call-data:
            * All call records and artifacts are removed.
            * Any remaining background jobs related to those calls must be canceled or become no-ops safely.
Account deletion:
            * Must revoke all sessions and devices.
            * Must delete all user-owned data according to the deletion model selected for Phase 1 (hard deletion is recommended for Phase 1 simplicity unless a legal retention requirement is introduced later).
6.2.7 Memory visibility and deletion
Memory is enabled by default in Phase 1.
Privacy rules
            * Memory items must be visible in-app.
            * Memory items must be deletable individually.
            * Users must be able to delete all memory at once.
            * Turning memory off must stop new memory creation and must offer a clear option to delete existing memory.
6.2.8 Internal support access boundaries
The admin portal is internal-only.
Privacy requirements
            * Portal roles must be least privilege by default.
            * Access to sensitive content (transcript, summary, recording) must be restricted and audited if it exists at all.
            * The default support workflow should rely on metadata and status, not reading user content.
________________


6.3 Reliability requirements
6.3.1 Reliability goals for Phase 1
Phase 1 must remain usable even when external vendors partially fail, networks degrade, or background jobs are delayed.
Key reliability outcomes:
            * Calls produce a call record even if summary fails.
            * Calls do not get stuck forever in “processing” without visibility.
            * Notifications are retried and delivery status is tracked.
            * Retention and deletion jobs cannot silently fail.
6.3.2 Availability targets (SLOs)
Because Phase 1 is deployed as Docker on a VM, targets must be realistic while still protecting user trust.
Service availability targets
            * Backend API availability: 99.5% monthly
            * Call handling endpoint availability (webhooks and call orchestration): 99.5% monthly
Degradation expectation
If the system is down:
            * Calls must receive a safe fallback response rather than dead air.
            * The fallback can be a short message that the user is unavailable and the caller should try later.
            * The system must alert operators immediately.
6.3.3 Failure tolerance and partial success rules
Phase 1 must treat call record creation as the top reliability priority.
Minimum durable output
            * Create call record with:
            * call timestamps
            * caller number (if available)
            * processing status fields
            * failure reason categories when downstream steps fail
Partial artifact rules
            * Transcript may be delayed or fail.
            * Summary may be delayed or fail.
            * Recording may fail even if enabled.
            * The UI must show explicit states (pending, ready, failed) and allow safe retries where applicable.
6.3.4 Retry policies and idempotency
Retry categories
            * Provider transient failures (timeouts, 5xx): retry with backoff.
            * Permanent failures (invalid number, invalid payload): no retry, mark failed with reason.
Idempotency requirements
            * Webhook handlers must be idempotent to prevent duplicates when providers retry.
            * Background job processors must be idempotent to prevent double summaries or double notifications.
6.3.5 Asynchronous processing reliability
To keep calls responsive, heavy tasks run asynchronously:
            * transcript post-processing
            * summary generation
            * label analysis
            * reminder triggers
            * retention deletion jobs
Queue requirements
            * Every async job must have:
            * a unique job id
            * retry count and next retry time
            * final dead-letter handling
            * observability: queue depth and failure rate monitoring
6.3.6 Notification reliability requirements
Notifications must have retry and delivery tracking.
Rules
            * All notification sends must be recorded with status:
            * queued, sent, delivered (if available), failed, retried, abandoned
            * Failure reasons must be categorized:
            * invalid token
            * provider rejected
            * rate limited
            * network error
            * Token invalidation must remove dead tokens to avoid infinite failures.
6.3.7 Backup and restore requirements
Even for Phase 1, recovery must be possible.
Database backups
            * Automated daily backups minimum.
            * Point-in-time recovery enabled if available.
            * Backup retention: minimum 14 days.
Object storage (recordings)
            * Rely on storage durability, but ensure deletion is enforceable.
            * If versioning is enabled, deletion must remove all versions for compliance with “real deletion.”
Restore testing
            * At least one restore rehearsal before go-live:
            * restore database to a new instance
            * verify app can read restored data
            * verify retention/deletion jobs still function
6.3.8 Operational alerts and incident readiness
Alerts are mandatory and must include:
            * failed calls spike
            * summary generation failure spike
            * transcript failure spike
            * queue backlog growth
            * cost spike indicators (call volume, AI usage)
Incident response basics required:
            * on-call ownership list
            * severity levels
            * rollback steps
            * “disable risky feature” steps (see feature controls below)
6.3.9 Operational feature controls (safe switches)
Because there is no staging and telephony is risky, Phase 1 must include operational switches that can reduce harm quickly.
Minimum switches (feature flags or configuration toggles):
            * disable recordings
            * disable live handoff
            * disable summary generation retries
            * disable AI drafted texts (still allow manual texting)
            * tighten rate limits temporarily
            * block abusive caller numbers at platform level (admin-only, audited)
________________


6.4 Performance requirements
6.4.1 Performance goals for Phase 1
The system must feel responsive during calls and must keep the app UI fast and stable.
Performance is measured in:
            * time to answer a call and start speaking
            * conversational latency during screening
            * time to produce transcript and summary artifacts
            * mobile UI responsiveness when opening call history and call detail
6.4.2 Call answer and greeting latency targets
Time to answer target
            * Target: start assistant greeting within 3 seconds after the call is answered by the platform.
            * Hard ceiling: 6 seconds. If exceeded, treat as degraded and alert if frequent.
6.4.3 Conversational latency targets (during screening)
Interactive latency target
            * Target: caller speech to assistant response within 1.5 seconds on average.
            * Acceptable ceiling: 3 seconds for occasional turns.
If latency exceeds ceiling:
            * The assistant should use a short filler response (for example: “One moment”) to avoid silence.
            * The call record must still be produced normally.
6.4.4 Summary and transcript processing targets
Because summary and transcript post-processing can be asynchronous:
Transcript readiness targets
            * 90% of calls: transcript ready within 2 minutes
            * 99% of calls: transcript ready within 10 minutes
Summary readiness targets
            * 90% of calls: summary ready within 2 minutes
            * 99% of calls: summary ready within 10 minutes
If targets are not met:
            * UI must clearly show “processing” state.
            * Background jobs must retry and operators must be alerted if backlog grows.
6.4.5 Mobile app responsiveness targets
Cold start
            * Target: home screen usable within 2 seconds on modern devices.
Call list
            * Target: call list loads within 500 ms after data is available locally or after fetch completes.
Call detail
            * Target: open call detail within 500 ms, then lazy-load transcript if needed.
Scrolling
            * Call history list must maintain smooth scrolling for at least 1,000 call records (even if Phase 1 retention is shorter, this prevents UI degradation).
6.4.6 API response time targets
For backend APIs (excluding heavy exports which are out of scope):
            * 95th percentile: under 300 ms
            * 99th percentile: under 800 ms
Heavy operations (summary generation, retention deletion) must not run in request threads.
________________


6.5 Scalability requirements
6.5.1 Scalability goals for Phase 1
Phase 1 must scale beyond the initial user count without redesigning the core architecture.
The goal is not infinite scale in Phase 1, but predictable scale:
            * handle early growth
            * handle spam bursts
            * handle background job spikes after call bursts
6.5.2 Concurrency targets (platform level)
Phase 1 must support at least:
            * 50 concurrent inbound call sessions across all users
            * 200 queued background jobs pending without user-visible failures
This target accounts for:
            * spam caller bursts
            * a subset of users receiving calls at the same time
            * delayed AI processing that increases backlog temporarily
6.5.3 Horizontal and vertical scaling expectations
Because deployment is Docker on a VM:
            * Backend services must be stateless where possible.
            * State must be stored in Postgres and the queue.
            * Scaling approach:
            * increase VM size (vertical) as the first step
            * increase worker count for async jobs
            * add additional VM instances later if needed (future enhancement) without changing core design
6.5.4 Database scalability requirements
            * Use proper indexes on user_id, created_at, caller_number, and status fields.
            * Keep call record writes efficient and avoid large synchronous transactions.
            * Use pagination for call history APIs.
            * Avoid N+1 query patterns in call list and admin portal queries.
6.5.5 Cost-scalability controls
To prevent cost spikes:
            * rate limit calls per caller number and per user
            * cap retries for expensive AI tasks
            * detect abusive patterns and throttle quickly
            * provide operator alerts for abnormal usage
________________


6.6 Maintainability requirements
6.6.1 Maintainability goals
With a small team and a two-month deadline, maintainability must be built in so Phase 1 can be shipped, supported, and extended.
Maintainability outcomes:
            * codebase is structured and navigable
            * changes can be deployed safely
            * failures are observable and diagnosable
            * schema changes are controlled and repeatable
            * future phases can build on the foundation without rewrites
6.6.2 Repository and code structure requirements
Recommended monorepo layout:
            * mobile/ (React Native app)
            * web-admin/ (Next.js internal portal)
            * backend/ (Python API and job workers)
            * realtime/ (Node.js telephony and streaming bridge)
            * infra/ (Docker, deployment scripts, environment templates)
            * docs/ (technical plan, runbooks, QA evidence templates)
6.6.3 Typed contracts and schema discipline
            * Backend APIs must have explicit request and response schemas.
            * Use OpenAPI generation for backend endpoints.
            * Shared types for event payloads (call events, notification events) must be versioned.
6.6.4 Database migrations and versioning
            * All schema changes must be performed through migrations.
            * Migrations must be applied automatically in CI for dev and in a controlled step for production.
            * Migrations must be backward compatible where possible to support safe rollout.
6.6.5 Testing as a maintainability requirement
Phase 1 requires:
            * unit tests for core logic
            * integration tests for APIs and job processing
            * end-to-end tests for:
            * dedicated number call flow
            * forwarding call flow
            * voicemail detection behavior
            * live handoff offer and accept
            * live handoff failure and loop prevention
            * approval-first texting
            * retention and deletion
Tests must run in CI and block merges when failing.
6.6.6 Observability and debugging requirements
Phase 1 uses self-hosted Sentry.
Requirements:
            * Every service must report errors with environment tags (dev or prod).
            * Releases must be tagged so regressions can be tied to deployments.
            * Sensitive content must not be sent to Sentry (redact transcripts, summaries, phone numbers where feasible).
            * Structured logs must exist for call lifecycle tracking using correlation ids:
            * call_id
            * user_id
            * provider_call_sid
            * job_id
6.6.7 Operational documentation requirements
Maintainability includes runbooks. Phase 1 must have:
            * deployment runbook
            * rollback runbook
            * incident response checklist
            * common forwarding troubleshooting guide
            * common handoff troubleshooting guide
            * retention and deletion verification checklist
            * secrets rotation checklist
6.6.8 Change control requirements
Because Phase 1 scope is strict:
            * Any change that adds user-visible features must be treated as a scope change and explicitly approved.
            * Non-functional fixes (security, reliability, performance) can be prioritized within scope because they protect the Phase 1 promise.
________________


CHAPTER 7: SYSTEM CONTEXT AND EXTERNAL INTEGRATIONS
7.1 System context diagram (textual)
Phase 1 is composed of five primary runtime zones:
            1. Caller (PSTN network)
            * Any external person dials a user’s dedicated AI number.
            * The call originates from a regular phone network (mobile or landline).
            2. Telephony layer (Twilio)
            * Owns the inbound phone number inventory.
            * Terminates the PSTN call and exposes programmable call control.
            * Connects the live call audio to the MattBot realtime voice service using Media Streams.
            * Sends webhook events (call status, recording events, messaging status) back to the MattBot backend.
            3. MattBot backend (AWS, Docker on VM)
            * API backend (Python): authentication, user settings, labels, contacts, call records, summaries, approvals, reminders, device registration, admin operations.
            * Realtime voice service (Node.js): websocket handling for Twilio Media Streams and realtime interaction with ElevenLabs for speech-to-speech behavior (low latency, streaming audio).
            * Async workers (Python): post-call processing, summarization, retention jobs, notification fanout, cost tracking, retry logic.
            * Data services (AWS managed): PostgreSQL (RDS), object storage (S3), queueing (SQS).
            4. AI voice layer (ElevenLabs)
            * Provides realtime voice agent capability for the call experience.
            * Provides STT output (transcripts) and can optionally provide summarization output when available.
            5. User-facing clients
            * Mobile app (iOS + Android) for personal users.
            * Web portal for the same account owner (desktop access), not for the caller.
            * Internal admin web console (restricted role) for support operations.
External connectivity is strictly hub-and-spoke:
            * Clients talk only to MattBot backend APIs.
            * Twilio talks to MattBot backend via webhooks and Media Streams.
            * MattBot backend talks outward to Twilio APIs, ElevenLabs APIs, push notification provider, and (if used) email delivery for OTP.
This chapter defines what those external connections are, what data crosses them, and the controls required to keep them safe and reliable.
________________


7.2 Integration inventory (what is integrated, and why)
7.2.1 Twilio (Telephony and Messaging)
Purpose
               * Provision and manage a dedicated inbound phone number per user.
               * Receive inbound calls and control call routing (AI screening, forwarding mode, live handoff).
               * Provide realtime call audio transport (Media Streams).
               * Send and receive SMS for the “approval-first texting” workflow.
               * Produce call lifecycle events needed for reliability, auditing, and billing.
Key Twilio products and features
               * Programmable Voice
               * Phone Numbers (provisioning and configuration)
               * Voice webhooks (call events)
               * Media Streams (bidirectional, for realtime voice bot audio path)
               * Programmable Messaging (SMS)
Trust boundary
               * Twilio is an external processor. The backend must validate all inbound webhook signatures and treat inbound webhook payloads as untrusted input.
________________


7.2.2 ElevenLabs (Realtime voice agent and STT)
Purpose
               * Realtime conversational agent behavior during calls (low-latency speech-to-speech).
               * Transcription output (STT) used to generate call records, summaries, labels, and message drafts.
               * Optional: summarization/extraction if ElevenLabs provides it in the chosen product tier and capability.
Trust boundary
               * ElevenLabs is an external AI processor. Audio and derived text are sensitive personal data. The system must minimize what is sent, store only what is needed, and apply retention and deletion rules consistently.
________________


7.2.3 Push notifications (FCM with APNS delivery for iOS)
Purpose
               * Deliver call-screening completion, message draft ready, approval required, reminder notifications.
               * Multi-device fanout for a single user account (all logged-in devices may receive notifications).
Privacy constraint
               * Default notification content is private-by-default:
               * Lock screen shows a generic message like “New call screened”.
               * No caller name, no transcript snippets, no message content.
               * The user must open the app and pass local device unlock to see details.
________________


7.2.4 Email delivery (only for Email OTP and account security emails)
Purpose
               * Email OTP (fallback MFA method) and account security emails (password reset, device sign-in alerts, change confirmations).
Decision for Phase 1
               * Use a transactional email provider that is reliable and supports deliverability basics (domain verification, SPF/DKIM). In AWS, the default choice is Amazon SES.
               * In development, a local capture tool (example: MailHog) is acceptable to avoid sending real email.
________________


7.2.5 Identity providers (Google Sign-In, Apple Sign-In)
Purpose
               * User authentication through OAuth/OpenID Connect for Google login.
               * Apple login is required for iOS compliance if other third-party social login is offered.
Scope note
               * Deep auth protocol details belong to the Authentication and Identity chapter; this chapter only defines the external trust boundary and the integration surface.
________________


7.2.6 Observability: self-hosted Sentry
Purpose
               * Centralized error reporting across mobile, web, backend, and realtime services.
               * Provides stack traces, release tracking, and alerting without exposing sensitive payloads.
Privacy constraint
               * No raw transcripts, recordings, phone numbers, or message contents should be sent to Sentry. Use structured redaction and hashing.
________________


7.3 Data exchanged per integration (direction, fields, and controls)
7.3.1 Twilio integration data flows
7.3.1.1 Inbound call initiation (Twilio → Backend)
Trigger
               * A caller dials a user’s dedicated AI number (Twilio number).
Transport
               * HTTPS webhook request from Twilio to the backend “voice inbound” endpoint.
Typical payload categories
               * Call identifiers: CallSid
               * Parties: From (caller), To (user AI number)
               * Call metadata: direction, timestamp, Twilio account identifiers
               * Optional: forwarding headers or SIP information (if applicable)
Backend responsibilities
               * Validate Twilio signature on every webhook.
               * Create or upsert a call session record keyed by CallSid.
               * Load user configuration by matching To number to the owning user.
               * Return TwiML instructions to connect the call to the realtime voice service.
Idempotency
               * Webhooks may be retried. The backend must treat CallSid as the idempotency key for call session creation and avoid duplicate sessions.
________________


7.3.1.2 Realtime call audio (Twilio ↔ Realtime voice service)
Trigger
               * The backend returns TwiML to start a realtime stream.
Transport
               * WebSocket between Twilio Media Streams and the Node.js realtime service.
Key implementation constraint
               * Unidirectional streams cannot send audio back to Twilio.
               * Bidirectional Media Streams require <Connect><Stream> so the server can send audio back into the call as media frames. (Twilio)
Audio format and framing
               * Twilio sends start then media messages with mulaw audio at 8 kHz, base64 encoded.
               * For bidirectional, the realtime service sends media messages back with mulaw audio at 8 kHz, base64 encoded, and can use clear to interrupt buffered audio. (Twilio)
Realtime service responsibilities
               * Maintain a per-call stream state machine (connected, started, streaming, draining, stopping).
               * Forward inbound audio to ElevenLabs realtime agent session.
               * Receive agent audio output and send back to Twilio as outbound media frames.
               * Track latency and buffering to keep conversation natural.
Failure handling
               * If the websocket drops:
               * Attempt one controlled reconnect path if Twilio retries.
               * If the call cannot be restored quickly, apply the configured fallback behavior (defined in the call flows chapter) and end the call gracefully.
________________


7.3.1.3 Call status, recording, and messaging events (Twilio → Backend)
Triggers
               * Call lifecycle events: ringing, answered, completed, failed.
               * Recording status events: recording started, completed, URL ready.
               * SMS events: queued, sent, delivered, failed.
Backend responsibilities
               * Validate Twilio signature.
               * Persist event timeline entries linked to the call record.
               * Mark the call record as completed and enqueue post-call processing.
               * If recording is enabled:
               * Store recording metadata, not the raw audio in logs.
               * Download or reference recording to store in S3 based on the retention policy.
               * Ensure consent rules were satisfied (announcement at call start if enabled).
Idempotency
               * Use event identifiers (when available) or a composite key (CallSid + event type + timestamp window) to avoid duplicated timeline entries.
________________


7.3.1.4 Call forwarding mode and live handoff (Twilio-managed call control)
Phase 1 includes:
               * Forwarding mode (required).
               * Live handoff (included) with handoff destination = user’s real phone number only.
This creates a critical loop-risk scenario:
Loop risk scenario
               * The user’s real phone number has carrier-level conditional forwarding (busy/unreachable) pointing to their AI number.
               * During live handoff, the system dials the user’s real number.
               * If the user is busy/unreachable, the carrier forwards that inbound handoff call back to the AI number, creating a new inbound call into the AI system.
               * If the AI system tries handoff again, an infinite loop is possible.
Best-practice loop prevention approach for Phase 1 (mandatory)
               1. Use a dedicated “handoff caller ID number” owned by the platform (not the original caller’s number).
               * This ensures that if the carrier forwards the handoff call back to the AI number, the inbound call shows From = handoff caller ID, making it reliably detectable.
               2. Implement inbound loop detection at call start
               * If inbound From is one of the platform-owned handoff caller ID numbers, immediately:
               * End the call with a short system message, or
               * Route to a dead-end TwiML that hangs up without invoking the AI.
               * Record a “handoff_loop_detected” event in the call timeline.
               3. Implement a per-call handoff attempt cap
               * Maximum handoff attempts per original call (example: 1 attempt).
               * After the cap, the system must fall back to “take a message” behavior and notify the user.
               4. Implement a short-lived loop guard
               * Store a TTL record keyed by (original CallSid, userId) for the handoff window.
               * If any inbound call is detected from the handoff caller ID within the TTL, block handoff logic immediately.
Forwarding rule selection
               * Support Busy + Unreachable forwarding triggers in Phase 1 because it covers the most common missed-call cases without requiring multiple user configurations.
________________


7.3.2 ElevenLabs integration data flows
7.3.2.1 Realtime agent session (Realtime service ↔ ElevenLabs)
Trigger
               * When Twilio stream starts, the realtime service creates or resumes an ElevenLabs realtime session tied to the call.
Transport
               * WebSocket or streaming HTTP depending on ElevenLabs agent API.
Data sent to ElevenLabs (minimum required)
               * Inbound audio frames (caller speech).
               * Agent configuration reference (voice, persona, call objective preset, memory rules).
               * Limited user context needed for the call (example: user name for greeting, VIP list decision, label rules).
Data received from ElevenLabs
               * Outbound audio frames (agent speech).
               * STT transcript segments (as the call progresses, if enabled).
               * Optional metadata: turn boundaries, confidence measures, detected intents.
Privacy control
               * Do not send entire user contact lists or long histories.
               * Send only the smallest context required to achieve the call objective.
________________


7.3.2.2 Post-call transcript and summary outputs (ElevenLabs → Backend, or Backend computed)
Trigger
               * Call completes and post-call processing starts.
Inputs
               * Either:
               * ElevenLabs STT transcript and summary outputs, or
               * Transcript from ElevenLabs STT plus summary computed by a separate model (only if required).
Outputs persisted
               * Transcript (structured, segmented).
               * Summary (short, structured).
               * Extracted fields required for the UI (reason, urgency, callback time suggestion).
               * Draft SMS message for approval-first texting workflow.
Retention alignment
               * Transcript, summary, and any recording references are governed by the system retention policy and deletion rules.
________________


7.3.3 Push notification integration data flows (Backend → Push provider → Devices)
Device registration (Client → Backend)
               * Device token (FCM token) plus device metadata:
               * platform (iOS/Android/web)
               * app version
               * device id
               * user id
               * Backend stores tokens per user and per device, with a last-seen timestamp.
Notification sending (Backend → Push provider)
               * Notification types:
               * Call screened complete
               * Approval required (text draft)
               * Reminder due
               * System security alert (new device sign-in)
               * Default payload policy:
               * Title/body are generic and safe for lock screen.
               * Deep-link route is included, but app requires unlock to view sensitive content.
Delivery tracking
               * Push provider delivery receipts are limited. The backend should track:
               * send attempt id
               * provider response
               * token invalidation events (remove dead tokens)
________________


7.3.4 Email integration data flows (Backend → Email provider)
Email OTP
               * Triggered when user selects Email OTP as MFA method, or as fallback.
               * Backend generates OTP code, stores a hashed OTP with short TTL, and sends the email.
Security emails
               * Password reset request
               * Device sign-in alerts
               * Account changes (email change, MFA change)
Minimum content rule
               * Never include transcripts, call contents, or caller personal details in emails.
________________


7.3.5 Identity provider integration data flows (Client ↔ Provider ↔ Backend)
Google Sign-In
               * Client obtains an ID token (OIDC).
               * Backend validates token signature and audience, then creates a session.
Apple Sign-In
               * Similar flow with Apple identity token validation.
               * Required for iOS if social login is present.
Session issuance
               * Backend issues platform session tokens and device bindings per the session policy (detailed in the authentication chapter).
________________


7.4 Integration-wide controls (applies to all external services)
               1. Secrets handling
               * All API keys and credentials are loaded at runtime via environment variables.
               * CI uses repository secret storage for build-time injection.
               * Secrets must never be committed to source control.
               2. Network egress control
               * Backend egress is restricted to known external endpoints:
               * Twilio API, Twilio webhook responses
               * ElevenLabs API
               * Push provider endpoints
               * Email provider endpoints
               * Identity provider token validation endpoints
               3. Payload redaction
               * Logs must redact phone numbers, transcript text, message bodies, and tokens.
               * Store sensitive content only in the database or object storage with controlled retention.
               4. Retry and backoff
               * Webhook endpoints must be fast and non-blocking.
               * Long work is queued (SQS) and retried with exponential backoff.
               * External API calls use bounded retries and circuit-breaker style fallback where appropriate.
               5. Idempotency
               * Twilio webhooks and message status updates must be idempotent.
               * Post-call processing must be idempotent per call record.
               6. Versioning and change isolation
               * Twilio webhook payload parsing must tolerate additive fields.
               * ElevenLabs response parsing must be tolerant to new metadata.
               * The system stores a “provider payload version” snapshot with each call to enable safe migrations.
This completes the external integration surface definition for Phase 1 and establishes the exact trust boundaries and data flows that the system architecture, security, and schema chapters must support.
________________


Chapter 8: Technology Stack Decisions
8.1 Mobile Application Stack
8.1.1 Chosen approach
The Phase 1 mobile app will be built using React Native. This provides a single codebase for iOS and Android while keeping development speed high and allowing strong security controls on-device (biometric gating, encrypted local cache, secure token storage).
8.1.2 Languages and core libraries
               * TypeScript for the entire mobile codebase (strict type checking enabled).
               * UI framework: React Native standard components plus a lightweight UI kit only if needed (avoid heavy dependencies early).
               * Navigation: React Navigation.
               * Networking: a single API client wrapper (fetch or axios), with centralized auth and retry rules.
               * State management: keep it simple first (React Query for server state + minimal local state store).
