               * Forms and validation: schema-based validation (so the same rules can be reused on backend later if needed).
8.1.3 Minimum OS versions
               * iOS: 16+
               * Android: 10+
These versions reduce edge-case maintenance and align with modern security and notification capabilities.
8.1.4 Security-relevant mobile implementation choices
                  * Secure secret storage on device: store refresh tokens and sensitive session material only in OS-backed secure storage.
                  * Biometric gating: enforce biometric unlock before showing sensitive call content (transcripts, summaries, recordings, and other sensitive screens based on the selected policy).
                  * Local caching: if any call data is cached for UX, it must be encrypted and cleared on logout, device revoke, delete-all, and account deletion.
                  * No vendor secrets in the mobile app: Twilio credentials, ElevenLabs keys, signing secrets, and any privileged API keys must never ship inside the app bundle.
8.1.5 Push notifications on mobile
The mobile app will register one push token per device and support delivery to multiple devices per user. Notifications must obey privacy rules (lock screen shows only safe text by default) and quiet hours behavior (intensity rules).
________________


8.2 Admin Web Portal Stack
8.2.1 Chosen approach
The Phase 1 admin portal will be built using Next.js.
8.2.2 Portal purpose boundary
This portal is for internal admin and operational use for the team running the system. It is not a consumer-facing web dashboard for end users.
8.2.3 Key technical characteristics
                  * TypeScript, strict mode.
                  * Authentication enforced via the backend session model.
                  * Role-based access control handled server-side (Admin, Support, Viewer) and enforced in both UI and API.
                  * Audit trails for high-risk actions initiated from the portal (feature flags, user/device actions, deletion operations).
________________


8.3 Backend Services Stack
8.3.1 Service split and rationale
Phase 1 uses two backend services with clear responsibility boundaries:
                  1. Core backend API in Python using FastAPI for the majority of business logic, data model, user settings, security controls, and API endpoints.
                  2. Realtime media and streaming bridge in Node.js for call audio streaming and low-latency realtime coordination, because realtime websocket and streaming paths are typically simpler and more robust to implement in Node’s ecosystem.
8.3.2 Python backend (FastAPI) responsibilities
                  * Auth, sessions, MFA enforcement, device registration and revoke.
                  * User settings and schedules (quiet hours, business hours, privacy mode, retention, memory rules).
                  * VIP list, block list, spam label overrides, caller normalization rules.
                  * Call record lifecycle (metadata, transcript storage, summary storage, labels, actions, reminders).
                  * Webhook endpoints for Twilio events and other vendor callbacks.
                  * Queue producer and job coordination (enqueue transcription post-processing, summary generation, retention deletion jobs).
                  * Admin portal APIs (RBAC protected).
8.3.3 Node.js realtime bridge responsibilities
                  * Twilio realtime audio path handling (media stream ingestion and forwarding).
                  * Low-latency bridging between telephony audio and the voice provider streaming API.
                  * Emitting call-state events to the Python service and to connected mobile clients (via websockets) when needed.
8.3.4 AI providers used in Phase 1
                  * Voice provider: ElevenLabs.
                  * Speech-to-text: ElevenLabs STT is the chosen approach.
                  * Summarization and extraction: implement via an internal provider adapter. If ElevenLabs built-in summarization meets requirements, use it first. If not, the adapter can switch to an external LLM provider without rewriting the call record pipeline.
Phase 1 must always produce a call record even when some AI outputs fail, with missing parts clearly marked.
________________


8.4 Data and Infrastructure Stack
8.4.1 Cloud and hosting model
                     * Cloud provider: AWS
                     * Deployment style: Docker on a VM (EC2)
No Kubernetes and no serverless-first architecture for Phase 1.
8.4.2 Data stores and messaging
                        * Primary database: RDS PostgreSQL
Used for all transactional data and audit trails, with strict per-user scoping rules enforced at the API layer.
                        * Queue system: AWS SQS
Used for async jobs that must retry safely (transcript post-processing, summary generation retries, retention deletion jobs, reminder triggers).
                        * File storage: S3
Used for recordings when enabled, and any other large artifacts. No public buckets, access only via authenticated, time-limited delivery strategy (signed URLs or proxy).
8.4.3 Environments
                           * Development
                           * Production
No staging environment will be used in Phase 1. This increases release risk for telephony changes, so the technical plan must include strong mitigations (separate dev Twilio accounts/numbers, strict rollout controls, and feature flags).
________________


8.5 CI/CD, Repo Strategy, and Secrets Handling
8.5.1 CI/CD tool
                              * GitHub Actions is the CI/CD runner for backend and portal builds, test execution, and deployment automation.
8.5.2 Repository strategy
Use a single repository (monorepo) with clear packages:
                              * mobile/ (React Native app)
                              * portal/ (Next.js admin portal)
                              * backend/ (FastAPI core service)
                              * realtime/ (Node.js media bridge)
                              * infra/ (IaC and deployment scripts)
                              * docs/ (technical plan artifacts, diagrams, runbooks)
This reduces drift between services, supports shared types/contracts, and makes coordinated changes (API + app) easier within the 2 month timeline.
8.5.3 Branching model
                              * Trunk-based with short-lived feature branches.
                              * PR reviews required for mainline merges.
                              * Every merge runs tests and lint checks.
                              * Releases are cut via tags and a controlled deployment workflow.
8.5.4 Secrets management strategy
The Phase 1 secrets strategy is intentionally cost-sensitive:
                              * Local development: .env files, gitignored, stored only on developer machines with proper OS permissions.
                              * CI/CD: GitHub Secrets used to store deployment credentials and environment secrets.
                              * Production VM: deploy-time write of secrets to protected env files on the server (owned by root or the service user, permission 600, never in the repo).
Mobile builds must never include privileged secrets. The app only stores user session tokens and device identifiers, never vendor keys.
8.5.5 Observability tooling decision
                              * Sentry self-hosted is the chosen error tracking and exception aggregation approach for Phase 1.
8.5.6 Feature flags
Feature flags will be implemented in Phase 1 to reduce risk without requiring a new mobile build and to allow fast shutdown of expensive or risky paths. Minimum flags include toggles for recordings, live handoff, text connector, advanced spam detection, and summary retry behaviors.
________________


CHAPTER 9: HIGH-LEVEL SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE
9.1 Component architecture diagram
9.1.1 Architecture goals for Phase 1
The Phase 1 architecture is designed to:
                              * Support inbound AI call handling with two entry points:
                              * Calls to a dedicated AI number
                              * Calls forwarded from the user’s personal number when busy or unreachable
                              * Keep the real-time call path low-latency by separating audio streaming concerns from the core API and business logic
                              * Ensure that both inbound entry points produce identical outputs and stored artifacts, differing only by a stored “source type” on the call record
                              * Make call handling stateful and auditable via explicit call state transitions, so failures are diagnosable and user experience stays consistent across retries and multiple devices
                              * Offload non-real-time work into asynchronous jobs (summaries, retention deletions, reminders, spam analysis) so the real-time path stays responsive
9.1.2 Primary components (Phase 1)
Phase 1 is composed of these cooperating components:
                              * Mobile app (React Native)
                              * Admin portal (Next.js, internal operator portal only)
                              * Core backend API (Python FastAPI) as the system of record and business logic host
                              * Realtime media gateway (Node.js) for real-time streaming and media bridge responsibilities
                              * PostgreSQL (AWS RDS) as the primary datastore
                              * S3 for media/object storage (recordings if enabled, large artifacts, and securely served assets)
                              * SQS for job queueing and background processing reliability
                              * Push notification delivery (FCM + APNS pattern)
                              * External providers:
                              * Twilio for Voice and SMS
                              * ElevenLabs for voice plus speech-to-text (STT)
9.1.3 Component diagram (logical)
flowchart LR
  Caller[Caller] --> Twilio[Twilio Voice]
  UserPhone[User personal number] -->|busy/unreachable forward| Twilio


  Twilio -->|webhook events| API[Core Backend API<br/>Python FastAPI]
  Twilio -->|media stream| Media[Realtime Media Gateway<br/>Node.js]


  Mobile[Mobile App<br/>React Native] <-->|HTTPS + WebSocket events| API
  Admin[Admin Portal<br/>Next.js] <-->|HTTPS| API


  API <-->|control + session| Media
  Media <-->|voice + STT streaming| EL[ElevenLabs]


  API --> DB[(PostgreSQL RDS)]
  API --> S3[(S3 Storage)]
  API --> Q[(SQS Queues)]


  Worker[Background Workers<br/>Python] <-->|consume jobs| Q
  Worker --> DB
  Worker --> S3


  API --> Push[Push Delivery<br/>FCM/APNS]
  API --> SMS[Twilio SMS<br/>approval-first]
9.1.4 Deployment note (high level only)
                              * Services are deployed as Docker workloads on AWS EC2 (Docker on VM), with dev and production environments only.
                              * Network topology, VPC layout, and security groups are specified in Chapter 10 (this chapter only defines the logical architecture boundary).
________________


9.2 Responsibility boundaries
This section defines what belongs in Python vs Node, and what belongs in clients vs backend, to avoid duplication and reduce real-time risk.
9.2.1 Python FastAPI (core backend) responsibilities
Python is the system of record and hosts the “backend brain” modules. It owns:
                              * Identity and session lifecycle:
                              * Authentication
                              * Device registration and revoke
                              * Session termination and per-device control
                              * Settings and policies:
                              * Versioned settings schema
                              * Schedule validation (quiet hours, business hours)
                              * Server-enforced policy checks
                              * Lists and caller policy:
                              * VIP list, block list, spam preference rules
                              * Number normalization and matching logic
                              * Call orchestration (the core call state machine driver):
                              * Receives call events (from Twilio webhooks)
                              * Starts and coordinates the AI call flow
                              * Tracks timers, transitions, and handoff eligibility
                              * Conversation engine (policy and script logic):
                              * Screening questions and branching behavior
                              * Special flows (VIP, blocked, unknown, sales)
                              * Call length limits enforcement
                              * Output generation:
                              * Transcript artifacts, summary, extracted fields
                              * Labels plus label reasons for transparency
                              * Call record creation and partial/failure marking
                              * Actions module:
                              * Text drafting, approval-first enforcement, send tracking
                              * Reminder creation and triggering mechanics
                              * Notification module:
                              * Push notifications
                              * Privacy mode enforcement
                              * Quiet hours enforcement for notifications
                              * Media module (authorization and lifecycle, not the real-time transport):
                              * Recording metadata, secure access control, retention deletion policies
                              * Audit and monitoring events (security relevant actions):
                              * Handoff events (offered, accepted, failed, expired)
                              * Device events, login events, sensitive setting changes, deletions
9.2.2 Node.js realtime media gateway responsibilities
Node.js is dedicated to real-time streaming and low-latency bridging tasks. It owns:
                              * Real-time audio transport bridging between:
                              * Twilio media streaming (or equivalent voice stream mechanism)
                              * ElevenLabs voice and ElevenLabs STT streaming
                              * Real-time session continuity:
                              * Connection management for the media stream
                              * Resilience to transient network errors (reconnect strategies where supported)
                              * Minimal state in the media layer:
                              * The Node service does not become the source of truth for call state
                              * It reports events to the Python orchestrator, which records state transitions and user-visible outcomes
9.2.3 Client responsibilities (mobile app and admin portal)
Mobile app responsibilities
The mobile app is a presentation and interaction layer only. It owns:
                              * Authentication UX, MFA UX, and device registration flows (as directed by backend policies)
                              * Call history viewing and filtering
                              * Call details viewing (gated by biometric rules where configured)
                              * Accept/decline handoff prompts (one tap flow)
                              * Approve-first texting UX (edit, approve, send)
                              * Settings UX (privacy mode, quiet hours, lists, retention, memory on or off)
The mobile app does not own:
                              * Any authoritative call state transitions
                              * Any business logic decisions about VIP/blocked/spam beyond rendering backend decisions
                              * Any local persistent storage of sensitive artifacts unless explicitly designed, encrypted, and covered by cache-clearing rules (handled in later security and data lifecycle chapters)
Admin portal responsibilities
The admin portal is an internal operations tool for the app operator (not a public user-facing web dashboard). It owns:
                              * Operational visibility:
                              * User lookup
                              * Call log viewing
                              * Forwarding diagnostics views
                              * Deletion tooling and support flows
                              * Role management for internal portal roles (admin/support/viewer)
It does not own:
                              * Core call handling logic
                              * AI conversation logic
                              * Any direct database manipulation without going through controlled backend endpoints (to preserve auditability)
9.2.4 Data ownership and “system of record”
                              * PostgreSQL is the system of record for all durable objects: users, devices, settings, lists, calls, transcripts, summaries, labels, actions, reminders, and audit logs.
                              * S3 stores large binary objects (recordings if enabled) and any large artifacts that should not live in DB tables.
                              * SQS holds transient work items with idempotent processing rules (defined in later job chapter), enabling safe retries for failures without blocking real-time call flows.
________________


9.3 Data flow overview
This subsection provides a single “map” of the end-to-end flow: call arrives, AI screening, transcript, summary, call record, notifications, and actions.
9.3.1 Inbound call flow (common steps for both entry points)
Step 1: Call arrives at Twilio
There are two supported inbound entry points:
                              * Direct: caller dials the user’s dedicated AI number
                              * Forwarded: caller dials the user’s personal number and the carrier forwards to the AI number on busy or unreachable
Step 2: Twilio notifies backend and starts the call session
                              * Twilio sends call initiation events to the core backend.
                              * The core backend creates the call record immediately and assigns:
                              * Call ID
                              * User owner
                              * Source type (direct vs forwarded)
                              * Initial call state (“call started”)
Step 3: Real-time audio path is established
During the call, the system must:
                              * Receive audio from telephony provider
                              * Convert caller speech to text for screening and transcript
                              * Convert assistant responses to speech for the caller
                              * Keep latency low enough to feel natural
This is achieved by:
                              * Twilio streaming audio to the realtime media gateway (Node)
                              * Node bridging to ElevenLabs for voice and STT
                              * Node reporting key events to Python orchestrator (call progress, handoff events, completion)
Step 4: Call orchestrator drives the conversation
The core backend orchestrator:
                              * Applies user settings (tone, objectives, call limits)
                              * Applies list policy (VIP vs blocked vs unknown)
                              * Tracks explicit state transitions:
                              * screening in progress
                              * handoff offered
                              * handoff accepted/expired
                              * call ended
9.3.2 Live handoff flow (high-level)
Live handoff requires the telephony integration to connect the caller to the user mid-call:
                              * Orchestrator decides eligibility and triggers handoff prompt
                              * Mobile app receives handoff prompt as a privacy-safe notification and/or in-app realtime event
                              * User accepts, backend executes the connect action through the telephony layer
                              * Backend records outcome (accepted, failed, expired) and continues conversation if handoff fails to prevent silence
Detailed sequencing, loop prevention, and timeout rules are handled in the dedicated Live Handoff Technical Design chapter later in the technical plan.
9.3.3 Post-call processing flow (asynchronous)
After the call ends, asynchronous work runs so the real-time path remains responsive:
                              * Transcript post-processing and finalization
                              * Summary generation and structured extraction
                              * Spam pattern analysis
                              * Retention deletion jobs
                              * Reminder triggers
A queue mechanism is required so tasks can retry safely and failures do not block the main flow.
9.3.4 Stored artifacts and required outputs
For each call, the system produces:
                              * Transcript with speaker separation
                              * Summary with strict formatting rules
                              * Extracted fields (minimum set):
                              * caller name
                              * reason
                              * callback number
                              * urgency
                              * optional best callback time
                              * Labels with label reasons for transparency
Failure handling rule:
                              * Even if an AI component fails, the system still creates a call record and marks missing parts clearly (no “silent loss” of the call).
9.3.5 Notifications and realtime updates (high-level)
Phase 1 supports realtime visibility for the user via:
                              * WebSocket events to the mobile app for live call events (call started, handoff offered, call ended, transcript ready, summary ready)
                              * Push notifications for key outcomes, respecting privacy mode defaults and quiet hours policies
9.3.6 Phase B and C readiness constraint
The architecture is explicitly structured so future phases do not require rebuilding foundations:
                              * Settings model is versioned and extensible
                              * Call record can evolve into a generic conversation record
                              * Approval-first actions pipeline is reusable for new outbound channels
                              * Notification system supports new event types and channels
                              * Connector pattern can be reused for other integrations
                              * Identity, device, and security systems remain stable across phases
________________


Chapter 10: AWS Network Architecture and Deployment Topology
10.0 Purpose and scope
This chapter defines the AWS network layout and how Phase 1 services are deployed and exposed to the internet. It covers:
                              * VPC design (subnets, routing, public vs private placement)
                              * HTTPS entry and TLS termination
                              * Security group rules per component
                              * Outbound egress design for external providers
                              * DDoS and WAF posture at the edge
                              * Environment separation for dev and production (no staging)
The goals are:
                              * Public internet exposure only where required (Twilio webhooks, mobile and web clients)
                              * Private placement for databases and internal services
                              * Clear, testable allow-list rules between components
                              * Simple deployment that fits “Docker on a VM” while staying secure and maintainable
________________


10.1 AWS account, region, and environment separation
10.1.1 Region selection
Phase 1 runs in a US region to match the Phase 1 launch market and reduce latency:
                              * Recommended region: us-east-1
                              * All resources in dev and production are created in the same region for operational simplicity.
10.1.2 Environment separation model (no staging)
Phase 1 has two environments only:
                              * dev
                              * prod
Separation rules:
                              * Dev and prod must use different AWS resources for all stateful services (RDS, S3 buckets, SQS queues).
                              * Dev and prod must use different credentials for all external providers (Twilio, ElevenLabs).
                              * Dev and prod must use separate DNS hostnames and separate TLS certificates.
                              * No dev traffic must ever use production numbers, production keys, or production databases.
Recommended structure (choose one and keep consistent):
                              * Option 1 (preferred if possible): separate AWS accounts for dev and prod.
                              * Option 2: single AWS account with separate VPCs and strict IAM boundaries between env namespaces.
10.1.3 Naming and tagging conventions
Every resource must include:
                              * app = mattbot
                              * env = dev|prod
                              * owner = team
                              * service = api|realtime|portal|sentry|db|queue|storage|lb
All DNS names must include env:
                              * api.dev.<domain>
                              * api.<domain>
                              * admin.dev.<domain>
                              * admin.<domain>
________________


10.2 VPC and subnet layout
10.2.1 VPC CIDR plan
Use one VPC per environment.
Example CIDRs:
                              * dev VPC: 10.10.0.0/16
                              * prod VPC: 10.20.0.0/16
10.2.2 Availability zones
Use at least 2 Availability Zones per environment for:
                              * load balancer placement
                              * database subnet groups
                              * future compute expansion
10.2.3 Subnet types and responsibilities
Each VPC includes:
Public subnets (2x)
                              * Contains the internet-facing load balancer
                              * Has a route to the Internet Gateway (IGW)
Private application subnets (2x)
                              * Contains EC2 instances running Docker workloads (API, realtime, portal, Sentry)
                              * Has no direct inbound from the internet
                              * Outbound internet access goes through NAT (see egress policy)
Private database subnets (2x)
                              * Contains RDS PostgreSQL
                              * No route to IGW
                              * Only reachable from application security groups on port 5432
10.2.4 Routing and gateways
Per VPC:
                              * Internet Gateway attached to the VPC
                              * NAT for outbound from private application subnets:
                              * Preferred: NAT Gateway for reliability
                              * Cost-optimized alternative: NAT instance (only if you accept the operational risk and manage patching)
Routing tables:
                              * Public route table: 0.0.0.0/0 -> IGW
                              * Private app route table: 0.0.0.0/0 -> NAT
                              * Private DB route table: no internet route
10.2.5 VPC endpoints (optional but recommended)
To reduce NAT usage and improve security posture, add VPC endpoints where appropriate:
                              * S3 Gateway Endpoint (for S3 access without public internet)
                              * SQS Interface Endpoint (optional, if you want queues fully private)
These endpoints reduce outbound exposure and reduce NAT costs for AWS-internal traffic.
________________


10.3 Public entry, load balancing, and TLS
10.3.1 DNS and domain layout
Use Route 53 (or your DNS provider) to map:
                              * api.<domain> -> public load balancer
                              * admin.<domain> -> public load balancer (restricted access)
                              * Optionally: sentry.<domain> -> public load balancer (restricted access)
Dev hostnames mirror prod with a dev prefix:
                              * api.dev.<domain>
                              * admin.dev.<domain>
                              * sentry.dev.<domain>
10.3.2 Load balancer choice
Use an Application Load Balancer (ALB) in public subnets for:
                              * HTTPS termination
                              * host-based routing (api vs admin vs sentry)
                              * path-based routing (api routes vs realtime routes)
                              * WebSocket support (for realtime events where used)
10.3.3 TLS strategy (HTTPS only)
TLS termination happens at the ALB using AWS Certificate Manager (ACM):
                              * Listener: 443 only
                              * Redirect HTTP 80 to HTTPS 443 (optional: keep 80 open only for redirect)
                              * Disable legacy TLS versions and prefer modern policies
Certificates:
                              * Issue separate certificates per environment hostname group (dev and prod)
                              * Keep the private keys inside ACM (never on EC2)
10.3.4 Request routing strategy
Two supported routing layouts. Pick one and standardize.
Layout A: host-based routing
                              * api.<domain> -> forwards to API target group
                              * admin.<domain> -> forwards to Portal target group
                              * sentry.<domain> -> forwards to Sentry target group
Layout B: single host with path-based routing
                              * /<api> -> API
                              * /admin/* -> Portal
                              * /sentry/* -> Sentry
Preferred for clarity: Layout A.
10.3.5 Target group design (Docker on a VM)
Phase 1 runs Docker on one EC2 per environment (initially). The ALB forwards to that EC2 instance.
Recommended inside the EC2 instance:
                              * A single reverse proxy (Nginx) listens on one internal port (example: 80)
                              * Nginx routes internally:
                              * /api/* -> FastAPI service port
                              * /realtime/* -> Node realtime service port (must support WebSocket upgrade)
                              * /admin/* -> Next.js admin portal port
                              * /sentry/* -> Sentry service port (if exposed)
Health checks:
                              * /health endpoint for API
                              * /health endpoint for realtime
                              * /health endpoint for portal
                              * Each must return 200 quickly and not depend on external providers.
10.3.6 Webhook exposure requirements
Twilio webhooks must reach the backend over the public internet:
                              * Twilio hits https://api.<domain>/twilio/...
                              * Because Twilio IP ranges are not guaranteed stable for allow-listing, the endpoint must remain public.
                              * Protection must rely on signature validation, schema validation, and rate controls (WAF and app-level rate limits).
________________


10.4 Compute deployment topology (Docker on EC2)
10.4.1 EC2 placement
EC2 instances run in private application subnets.
Inbound access to EC2 from the internet is not allowed.
Inbound access is only from:
                              * ALB security group (HTTP to reverse proxy)
                              * optional management channel (SSM) without opening SSH
10.4.2 Container workloads per environment
Minimum containers on the application EC2 instance:
                              * Reverse proxy (Nginx)
                              * API backend (Python FastAPI)
                              * Realtime media gateway (Node.js)
                              * Background workers (Python)
                              * Admin portal (Next.js)
                              * Self-hosted Sentry (web + worker + database dependency as per your chosen Sentry deployment model)
Operational rule:
                              * Realtime media gateway is separated as a distinct process and port to avoid coupling low-latency streaming with the API server.
10.4.3 Persistent storage on EC2
All durable application data must live in:
                              * RDS PostgreSQL
                              * S3 (recordings and large artifacts)
                              * SQS (jobs)
EC2 instance storage is treated as ephemeral except:
                              * Docker volumes required for Sentry (depending on how Sentry is deployed)
                              * Logs (temporary), rotated and shipped or pruned
________________


10.5 Database and internal service placement
10.5.1 RDS PostgreSQL subnet and exposure
RDS must be deployed in private database subnets with:
                              * publicly_accessible = false
                              * Security group that allows inbound 5432 only from the application security group
                              * No inbound from the ALB security group
                              * No inbound from the internet
10.5.2 S3 access model
S3 buckets are not in the VPC, but access is controlled by:
                              * Bucket policies that deny public access
                              * IAM roles on EC2 allowing only required bucket actions
                              * Optional VPC endpoint to keep traffic private
________________


10.6 Security groups policy (allow-list rules per component)
This section defines minimum inbound rules. Outbound rules are handled in the egress section.
10.6.1 ALB security group (public entry)
Inbound:
                              * TCP 443 from 0.0.0.0/0
                              * TCP 80 from 0.0.0.0/0 only if you implement HTTP to HTTPS redirect
Outbound:
                              * TCP 80 (or your chosen target port) to the application EC2 security group
10.6.2 Application EC2 security group (private app subnet)
Inbound:
                              * TCP 80 from ALB security group (reverse proxy)
                              * No other inbound from the internet
Management access (choose one):
                              * Preferred: no inbound SSH at all, use AWS SSM Session Manager
                              * If SSH is required: TCP 22 only from a fixed allow-list of operator IPs (never 0.0.0.0/0)
Outbound:
                              * To RDS SG on 5432
                              * To AWS services and external providers via NAT (defined in 10.7)
10.6.3 RDS security group (private DB subnet)
Inbound:
                              * TCP 5432 only from application EC2 security group
Outbound:
                              * default (managed by RDS), no custom inbound required
10.6.4 Admin portal exposure policy
The admin portal is internal-use only, but must be reachable by operators. Apply at least one of these network controls:
Option 1 (recommended): IP allow-list at the ALB/WAF layer
                              * Only allow your office IPs and known operator IPs to reach admin.<domain>
Option 2: require a VPN entry point
                              * Admin portal only accessible from inside VPN ranges
Option 3: strong authentication only
                              * Not preferred as the only control because it increases attack surface
Phase 1 should implement Option 1 minimum.
10.6.5 Sentry exposure policy
Self-hosted Sentry should not be broadly public. Apply one of:
                              * Only accessible from operator IP allow-list
                              * Only accessible via VPN
                              * Only exposed internally (no public DNS) if you can operate it that way
________________


10.7 Egress policy (outbound traffic)
10.7.1 Egress design objective
Private subnets must reach external providers:
                              * Twilio API
                              * ElevenLabs API
                              * Push notification endpoints (FCM and APNS paths)
                              * Google and Apple auth token validation endpoints (as required)
                              * Email provider endpoints if Email OTP is used
                              * OS package repositories for patching (if needed)
10.7.2 NAT-based egress
For private application subnets:
                              * All outbound internet traffic routes through NAT.
NAT placement:
                              * NAT lives in a public subnet with an Elastic IP.
10.7.3 Egress restriction level (Phase 1)
In Phase 1, strict domain-based egress allow-listing is not required because it adds complexity and cost. Instead, Phase 1 must implement:
                              * Strong inbound validation (signature checks for webhooks)
                              * App-level rate limiting
                              * VPC Flow Logs to observe unusual outbound patterns
                              * Least-privilege IAM so compromised workloads cannot access unrelated AWS resources
Optional hardening later:
                              * Egress proxy with allow-listed domains
                              * AWS Network Firewall (costly, not required for Phase 1)
________________


10.8 DDoS and WAF posture
10.8.1 Baseline DDoS protection
Use AWS Shield Standard (automatic for ALB) as baseline protection.
10.8.2 AWS WAF attachment
Attach AWS WAF to the ALB to protect:
                              * api.<domain> endpoints
                              * admin.<domain> endpoints
10.8.3 WAF rule groups (minimum)
Apply a baseline set:
                              * AWS Managed Common Rules
                              * Known bad inputs rules (SQLi/XSS patterns)
                              * Size restriction rules for request bodies and headers
                              * Bot control basic posture (if available within your cost tolerance)
10.8.4 Rate-based rules (critical for Phase 1)
Define rate-based WAF rules to reduce abuse and cost spikes:
                              * Rate limit on authentication endpoints (login, token refresh, MFA endpoints)
                              * Rate limit on Twilio webhook endpoints by source IP (coarse), plus app-level controls by caller number and user
                              * Rate limit on admin portal endpoints
Admin portal WAF restriction:
                              * Enforce IP allow-list for admin.<domain> and sentry.<domain> hostnames.
________________


10.9 Network logging and auditability
10.9.1 VPC Flow Logs
Enable VPC Flow Logs for:
                              * VPC
                              * Subnets (at minimum private application and private database subnets)
Retention:
                              * Keep at least 14 days in dev
                              * Keep at least 30 days in prod
Purpose:
                              * Detect unexpected inbound attempts
                              * Detect anomalous outbound patterns
                              * Support incident investigation
10.9.2 Load balancer access logs
Enable ALB access logs to S3 in each environment:
                              * Separate bucket prefix per env
                              * Deny public access
                              * Apply retention lifecycle rules (example: 30 to 90 days)
10.9.3 CloudTrail and IAM audit baseline
CloudTrail must be enabled for:
                              * IAM changes
                              * security group changes
                              * route table changes
                              * RDS changes
                              * S3 policy changes
This is required to trace infrastructure changes and support incident review.
________________


10.10 Reference topology diagram (logical network)
flowchart TB
  Internet[Public Internet] --> ALB[ALB Public Subnets]
  ALB -->|HTTP to Reverse Proxy| EC2[EC2 Private App Subnets\nDocker: Nginx + FastAPI + Node + Workers + Portal + Sentry]
  EC2 -->|5432| RDS[(RDS PostgreSQL\nPrivate DB Subnets)]
  EC2 -->|S3 API| S3[(S3 Buckets)]
  EC2 -->|SQS API| SQS[(SQS Queues)]
  EC2 -->|Outbound via NAT| NAT[NAT Gateway or NAT Instance\nPublic Subnet]
  NAT --> Internet2[Public Internet\nTwilio, ElevenLabs, Push, Auth, Email]

________________
10.11 Deployment topology per environment (dev and prod)
10.11.1 Dev environment topology
Dev runs the same component layout as prod but is cost-optimized:
                              * Separate VPC and subnets
                              * Separate RDS instance
                              * Separate S3 buckets and SQS queues
                              * Separate Twilio and ElevenLabs credentials
                              * Separate DNS and TLS certs
Traffic rules:
                              * Dev endpoints are publicly reachable only as needed (Twilio webhooks still need public reachability).
                              * Admin and Sentry access are restricted by IP allow-list.
10.11.2 Prod environment topology
Prod uses the same layout with stricter controls:
                              * Stronger WAF rules and tighter rate limits
                              * Full logging enabled (ALB logs, Flow Logs, CloudTrail)
                              * Restricted admin and Sentry access (IP allow-list required)
                              * Backup and restore readiness for RDS (details in backup chapter)
________________


10.12 Operational access and hardening (network-relevant)
10.12.1 Management access without public SSH (preferred)
Use AWS Systems Manager (SSM) for:
                              * shell access to EC2 without opening inbound ports
                              * run command for operational tasks
                              * patching workflows
This removes the need for inbound SSH and reduces attack surface.
10.12.2 If SSH is used (fallback)
If SSH must be enabled:
                              * Only allow TCP 22 from fixed allow-listed IPs
                              * Enforce key-based auth only
                              * Disable password login
                              * Rotate keys and remove unused keys promptly
10.12.3 Minimal inbound exposure summary
Phase 1 allows public inbound only at the ALB:
                              * HTTPS 443 for mobile app, web portal, Twilio webhooks
                              * HTTP 80 only for redirect (optional)
Everything else is private and allow-listed through security groups.
________________


Chapter 11: Identity, Authentication, and Session Model
11.1 Login methods (Phase 1)
11.1.1 Supported login methods
Phase 1 supports the following login methods for end users:
                              1. Email + password
                              2. Google Sign-In (OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect)
                              3. Sign in with Apple (iOS only)
Notes for iOS compliance:
                              * If the app ships Google Sign-In on iOS, Sign in with Apple must also be offered on iOS. This avoids a common App Store rejection risk when third-party social login exists.
11.1.2 Canonical identity model
                              * A “User” is the canonical account object.
                              * A user can have one or more “Auth Identities” linked to the same user:
                              * email_password identity (email)
                              * google identity (provider user id, plus email if available)
                              * apple identity (provider user id, plus email if available)
Linking rules:
                              * If a user signs in with a provider that returns an email that already exists, the system links to the existing user after proving control of that email (see 11.1.4).
                              * Provider user id (the stable subject identifier) is the primary key for OAuth identities, not email.
                              * Email is unique across all users.
11.1.3 Email verification requirements
Email verification is required before the account is considered “fully active.”
                              * Email + password signup:
                              * Create user in “pending verification” state.
                              * Send verification email with a short-lived token.
                              * Until verified:
                              * Login is allowed only to finish verification and MFA enrollment.
                              * Risky actions are blocked (example: provisioning a phone number, enabling forwarding, enabling texting, viewing sensitive call content).
                              * Google and Apple:
                              * Treat email as verified if the provider indicates verified email.
                              * If provider does not guarantee verification, require email verification step.
11.1.4 Account linking and takeover prevention
To prevent account takeover via “same email” collisions:
                              * If an OAuth login returns an email that already exists:
                              * If that email is already linked to the same provider subject, allow login.
                              * Otherwise, require a linking proof:
                              * User must authenticate with the existing method (password + MFA, or existing provider + MFA) before linking the new identity.
                              * Do not auto-link identities purely by email without proof.
11.1.5 Password policy (email + password)
Password rules should prioritize real security and usability:
                              * Minimum length: 12 characters
                              * Maximum length: 128 characters
                              * No forced complexity rules (no “must include symbols”)
                              * Block common and breached passwords (use an offline “top common passwords” list at minimum)
                              * Store passwords using Argon2id:
                              * Per-user random salt
                              * Parameters set to a “slow enough” standard for the deployment hardware
                              * Password resets:
                              * Always invalidate existing sessions (see 11.4.2)
                              * Always require MFA after reset before enabling sensitive actions
11.1.6 Login rate limiting and lockout
Phase 1 must include abuse controls at login:
                              * Per-account rate limit:
                              * Example: 5 failed attempts within 10 minutes, then temporary lock for 15 minutes
                              * Per-IP rate limit:
                              * Example: 20 attempts within 10 minutes, then temporary IP block with exponential backoff
                              * Add jitter and uniform error messages:
                              * “Invalid credentials” should not reveal whether the email exists
                              * Audit log entries for:
                              * repeated failed attempts
                              * lockouts triggered
                              * suspicious IP patterns
11.1.7 Device-first session creation (ties to multi-device requirement)
Phase 1 explicitly allows one user to be logged in on multiple devices at the same time.
                              * Every login creates or updates a device record and an associated session.
                              * Each device has its own refresh token and can be revoked independently.
________________


11.2 Multi-factor authentication (MFA) requirements
11.2.1 MFA is mandatory in Phase 1
MFA is required for all users.
Allowed MFA methods in Phase 1:
                              1. TOTP via authenticator app (required primary)
                              * Microsoft Authenticator, Google Authenticator, etc.
                              2. Email OTP (allowed only as a recovery fallback, weaker)
SMS OTP is not included by default (cost and SIM-swap risk). If added later, it must be optional and clearly labeled as weaker than TOTP.
11.2.2 MFA enrollment flow (required on first-time setup)
After initial account creation and email verification:
                              1. Prompt user to enroll MFA immediately.
                              2. Show QR code and secret for TOTP.
                              3. User enters a 6-digit code to confirm.
                              4. System marks MFA as enabled.
                              5. System generates recovery codes (see 11.2.3).
Blocking rule:
                              * Until MFA enrollment is completed, the user cannot finish onboarding (example: cannot provision numbers, cannot enable forwarding, cannot access call history screens).
11.2.3 Recovery codes (required)
Recovery codes are the primary “lost authenticator” path in Phase 1.
Rules:
                              * Generate a set of one-time use codes (example: 8 to 12 codes).
                              * Display once at enrollment and allow secure re-display only after step-up auth (password + TOTP).
                              * Store only salted hashes of recovery codes server-side.
                              * Each code can be used once, then invalidated and recorded in audit logs.
11.2.4 Step-up authentication triggers
Even after login, certain actions should require step-up MFA to reduce damage from stolen sessions:
Step-up required for:
                              * Changing password
                              * Changing email
                              * Enabling or disabling recording
                              * Changing notification privacy mode (because it affects lock screen leakage)
                              * Viewing or exporting sensitive items if “gate everything sensitive” is enabled (transcripts, recordings, caller details)
                              * Viewing recovery codes
                              * Revoking devices / “sign out all devices”
                              * Account deletion
11.2.5 Email OTP recovery rules (weaker fallback)
Email OTP is permitted only for account recovery and only with additional checks:
                              * Preconditions:
                              * User must know the password (or use a verified OAuth identity) plus email OTP.
                              * After successful recovery:
                              * Force new TOTP enrollment.
                              * Invalidate all sessions.
                              * Notify user on all devices (push) and by email.
________________


11.3 Session model and device management
11.3.1 Token types and transport
Phase 1 uses two token types:
                              * Access token: short-lived, used on every API request
                              * Refresh token: longer-lived, used to obtain new access tokens
Transport:
                              * Mobile: Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
                              * Web admin portal:
                              * Either Bearer tokens or secure httpOnly cookies
                              * Recommendation: use httpOnly cookies for refresh token on web to reduce XSS risk
11.3.2 Token storage rules (mobile)
Mobile apps must store tokens only in OS-backed secure storage:
                              * iOS: Keychain
                              * Android: Keystore-backed encrypted storage
Prohibited:
                              * Plain AsyncStorage for refresh tokens
                              * Shipping any secrets embedded in the app binary
11.3.3 Token lifetime and rotation (Phase 1 defaults)
Recommended defaults (balanced security + usability):
                              * Access token TTL: 15 minutes
                              * Refresh token TTL: 30 days rolling
                              * Absolute session maximum age: 90 days
                              * Refresh rotation: required
                              * On every refresh, issue a new refresh token and invalidate the old one
Revocation behavior:
                              * Revoking a device must immediately invalidate its refresh token.
                              * Access tokens remain valid only until expiry (15 minutes). If immediate cut-off is required for a specific event (example: account deletion), deny all tokens by user status check.
11.3.4 Session invalidation rules
Events that must invalidate sessions:
                              * Password reset: invalidate all sessions
                              * MFA reset: invalidate all sessions
                              * Account deletion: invalidate all sessions immediately
                              * Device revoke: invalidate only that device’s sessions
                              * Security flag triggered (abuse detection): invalidate all sessions optionally, depending on severity
11.3.5 Session and device data model requirements
Minimum backend objects to support the model:
Device
                              * device_id (server-generated)
                              * user_id
                              * platform (ios/android/web)
                              * device_name (user-friendly label)
                              * app_version, os_version (for debugging and security policy)
                              * push_token (nullable, updateable)
                              * last_seen_at
                              * created_at
                              * revoked_at (nullable)
                              * revoke_reason (nullable)
Session
                              * session_id
                              * user_id
                              * device_id
                              * access_token_hash (or access token id)
                              * refresh_token_hash
                              * access_expires_at
                              * refresh_expires_at
                              * created_at
                              * last_refresh_at
                              * revoked_at (nullable)
                              * revoked_reason (nullable)
                              * ip_at_creation, last_ip (optional but useful for abuse detection)
                              * user_agent (optional)
Storage rules:
                              * Store only hashes of tokens server-side, never plaintext.
                              * Use constant-time comparisons for token hash checks.
11.3.6 Multi-device rules
Default behavior:
                              * Multiple devices can be logged in at the same time.
                              * By default, all devices receive notifications.
Minimum device management features in Phase 1:
                              * List signed-in devices
                              * Revoke a device
                              * “Sign out of all devices”
                              * Rename device label (optional but highly useful)
Lost device scenario:
                              * Revoking a device must:
                              * stop future refreshes
                              * remove/disable its push token server-side
                              * be recorded in audit logs
                              * trigger user notification on remaining devices
11.3.7 Backend authorization check sequence
Every API request must follow a consistent check order:
                              1. Validate access token format and signature (if JWT) or lookup (if opaque)
                              2. Verify session is not revoked and not expired
                              3. Verify user is active (not deleted, not locked)
                              4. Enforce role/permission checks (admin portal vs personal user)
                              5. Enforce privacy gates and step-up requirements (MFA, biometrics gating policies)
________________


11.4 Account recovery and safety flows
11.4.1 Password reset
Password reset flow (email + password accounts):
                              1. User requests reset by email.
                              2. Send reset token (short-lived).
                              3. User sets new password.
                              4. Require MFA step after reset (TOTP or recovery code).
                              5. Invalidate all existing sessions.
                              6. Notify user (email + push where possible).
Security properties:
                              * Reset tokens must be single-use.
                              * Reset tokens must expire quickly (example: 15 minutes).
                              * Rate limit reset requests per email and per IP.
11.4.2 Lost authenticator recovery
Primary recovery path:
                              * Use recovery code to sign in and re-enroll TOTP.
Fallback recovery path (only if no recovery codes remain):
                              * Email OTP + existing password (or existing OAuth login) + risk checks
                              * Force MFA reset and re-enrollment
                              * Invalidate all sessions
                              * Notify user on all channels available
11.4.3 Email change
Email change is allowed only with step-up:
                              * Require TOTP (or recovery code) before changing email
                              * Require verification of the new email before it becomes active
                              * Notify old email address that a change was attempted/completed
11.4.4 Security notifications
Phase 1 must notify users for critical identity events:
                              * New device login
                              * Device revoked
                              * Password changed
                              * MFA enabled/disabled/reset
                              * Account deletion requested/completed
Notification content must respect privacy mode defaults:
                              * Lock screen should not include sensitive context, only a generic alert that something changed.
________________


11.5 Completion checklist for this chapter
This chapter is complete when all of the following are true in dev and production:
                              * Users can sign up and sign in with email + password, Google, and Apple on iOS.
                              * Email verification works and blocks risky onboarding actions until verified.
                              * MFA enrollment is mandatory and cannot be skipped.
                              * Recovery codes work and are stored safely (hashed, one-time use).
                              * Sessions refresh correctly with rotation and proper expiry.
                              * Users can see and revoke devices, including “sign out all devices.”
                              * Password reset invalidates all sessions and forces MFA step.
                              * Identity events are recorded in audit logs and trigger safe notifications.
                              * No endpoint returns another user’s data under any circumstances (verified via negative tests and QA).
________________


Chapter 12: Authorization and Access Control
12.1 Consumer app authorization (end user)
12.1.1 Goal and baseline rule
Phase 1 is a personal-user product, so the authorization model is strict per-user isolation: a signed-in user can only access their own data and can never access another user’s data, even if they guess an ID or tamper with requests. This is mandatory because Phase 1 handles personal communications and sensitive call content.
12.1.2 Identity context used for authorization
Every consumer API request is authorized using the identity from the session access token (created in the authentication and session model). The backend must treat the token’s user identifier as the only source of truth for “who is calling this API.” The app must never be allowed to supply a user_id that changes the authorization context.
Implementation rule
                              * All consumer endpoints must derive actor_user_id from the validated access token.
                              * Any user_id in path/query/body is ignored for authorization decisions (it can be used only as a filter if it equals actor_user_id, otherwise it is rejected).
12.1.3 Resource ownership model (how per-user isolation is enforced)
All user-owned records must include an owner_user_id (or equivalent) column. Authorization becomes a simple invariant:
Invariant: a consumer request may read or mutate a resource only if resource.owner_user_id == actor_user_id.
This must apply to every Phase 1 object type mentioned in the data model overview, including users, devices, numbers, calls, transcripts, summaries, recordings, labels, lists, reminders, messages, approvals, audit events, and handoff events.
Required backend behavior
                              * List endpoints must always filter by owner_user_id = actor_user_id.
                              * Read/update/delete endpoints must first fetch the resource by its internal ID and verify ownership before returning any data or applying any change.
                              * “Not found” vs “forbidden” behavior: return a generic not-found for non-owned IDs to avoid confirming that another user’s data exists.
12.1.4 Consumer permissions by object type (ownership rules)
Below are the minimum ownership and access rules that must be enforced server-side.
A) User profile and settings
                              * User can read/update only their own profile and settings.
                              * Settings changes that are privacy-critical must create an audit event (see audit requirements).
B) Devices and sessions (multi-device)
Phase 1 supports one account on multiple devices, and device removal must instantly remove access for that device.
Server-side rules:
                              * A device record belongs to exactly one user (owner_user_id).
                              * A device can only read its own session and device metadata, and can only update its own push token and device label.
                              * A user can list all their devices and revoke any device belonging to them.
                              * Revoking a device must invalidate that device’s refresh tokens and access tokens immediately.
C) Calls, transcripts, summaries, recordings
These are the most sensitive objects.
Call records:
                              * User can list/read only their own call records.
                              * User can delete a call record (per-call deletion is required).
Transcripts and summaries:
                              * Stored as objects owned by the same owner_user_id as the call record.
                              * Retrieval must be allowed only if the call is owned by the user.
Recordings:
                              * Recording is off by default; if enabled, playback must be protected in-app behind biometrics.
                              * Server-side access must still be enforced by ownership. Biometrics do not replace server-side authorization.
                              * Do not expose raw storage paths. Provide access only via one of these controlled patterns:
                              1. Backend proxy endpoint that streams the file after authorization, or
                              2. Short-lived signed URL minted only after ownership check.
D) Labels, VIP list, block list, spam markers
                              * Lists and labeling data are owned by the user and can be created/updated/deleted only by that user.
                              * The call record UI includes VIP/spam badges and reversible marking, so the backend must allow updating labels and list membership only for owned objects.
E) Reminders and follow-up actions
                              * Reminders must belong to the user and link to a call record owned by the same user.
                              * User can create/update/delete only their reminders.
                              * Action events (call back, notes, reminders, block/spam changes) must be stored in a call activity timeline and must never leak across users.
F) Messages and approval-first texting
Phase 1 is approval-first for outbound texts by default, so “draft,” “approval,” and “sent” states are user-owned and must not be accessible cross-user.
Server-side rules:
                              * Only the user can view/edit/approve their drafts.
                              * Only the user can trigger “send” after approval.
                              * Any failure and retry history must remain scoped to the same user.
G) Memory items
Memory is on by default, can store caller names/preferences, and must be visible and deletable in-app.
Server-side rules:
                              * Memory items are owned by the user.
                              * Provide endpoints to list, delete individual items, and wipe all memory for the user.
                              * Memory deletion must be complete (no shadow copies in secondary tables) and auditable.
H) Notifications and privacy mode
Private-by-default notification behavior is required: lock screen shows “New call screened” with no caller name or content; user opens the app and unlocks to see details.
Authorization rules:
                              * A push token belongs to a device and therefore to a user.
                              * Notification deep links must validate session authorization before showing call details.
12.1.5 Sensitive actions that must be audited
At minimum, the backend must generate audit events for login/logout, device add/remove, privacy-critical settings changes, data deletions, outbound text sent, and handoff events.
Authorization requirement:
                              * Audit records are append-only, owned by the user for consumer-visible audit history (if exposed later), and also available to internal ops with strict controls (admin section below).
                              * Audit logs must not store full transcript content.
12.1.6 Automated authorization test requirements
To prevent regressions:
                              * Add negative tests for every consumer endpoint: attempt to access another user’s object ID must return not-found.
                              * Add list filtering tests: listing calls/records must never include other users.
                              * Add deletion tests: delete must only affect owned resources and must not cascade into other users’ objects.
(These tests align with the security verification requirement to explicitly verify authorization and isolation.)
________________


12.2 Admin portal authorization (internal operator portal)
12.2.1 Portal scope and roles
Phase 1 includes an internal admin/operator portal, not a consumer web dashboard.
RBAC roles must exist:
                              * Admin
                              * Support
                              * Viewer
12.2.2 Admin identity separation
Admin portal identities must be separate from consumer identities. Do not reuse consumer sessions for admin access.
Required principles:
                              * Admin accounts are provisioned explicitly (no self-signup).
                              * Admin accounts must use MFA (TOTP required, email OTP allowed only as recovery).
12.2.3 RBAC permission model (what RBAC controls)
RBAC governs which administrative actions are allowed, independent of consumer ownership. Consumer ownership rules still apply when the portal “acts on behalf of” a user.
RBAC controls access to:
                              * User lookup and account status
                              * Number provisioning diagnostics (metadata only unless explicitly required)
                              * Call routing diagnostics (metadata)
                              * Content access (summaries/transcripts/recordings) with strict gating
                              * Deletion tools
                              * Role management
                              * Incident and reliability views
12.2.4 Exact permissions matrix (minimum Phase 1)
Permissions are grouped by capability. “Content” means any transcript, summary, note content, or recording.
Capability
	Viewer
	Support
	Admin
	View aggregate dashboards and operational metrics
	Yes
	Yes
	Yes
	Search users by email/phone and view account status
	No
	Yes
	Yes
	View device list and revoke device for a user
	No
	Yes
	Yes
	View call metadata (timestamps, durations, status codes, provider IDs)
	Yes (read-only)
	Yes
	Yes
	View user settings values (privacy-critical settings included)
	No
	Yes (read-only)
	Yes
	Change user settings
	No
	No
	Yes
	View call content (summary, transcript, notes)
	No
	Restricted (break-glass)
	Restricted (break-glass)
	Access recordings
	No
	No (Phase 1 default)
	Restricted (break-glass)
	Perform per-call delete for a user (on request)
	No
	Yes (requires reason)
	Yes
	Perform delete-all call data for a user
	No
	No
	Yes (requires reason + confirmation)
	Disable an account (security response)
	No
	Yes (requires reason)
	Yes
	Manage admin roles and portal users
	No
	No
	Yes
	Break-glass requirement (Phase 1)
Any access to call content must require:
                              * A reason code and free-text justification
                              * A time-limited elevated session flag (short TTL)
                              * An audit event that records who accessed what and why
This reduces unnecessary exposure of personal call data while still allowing troubleshooting when needed. It also aligns with the requirement for audit logging of sensitive events.
12.2.5 Content minimization defaults in the portal
Default portal views should show metadata only:
                              * For calls: status, timestamps, duration, call mode (dedicated AI number vs forwarded), urgency label, VIP/spam flags, and whether handoff was offered/accepted.
Content should not be displayed unless explicitly requested via break-glass.
12.2.6 Admin authorization enforcement points
                              * All portal API endpoints must require an admin access token.
                              * Every admin endpoint must check RBAC permissions server-side. Never rely on frontend hiding buttons.
                              * Every content access must also be ownership-scoped to a specific user and logged as an audit event.
________________


12.3 Internal service-to-service authentication (Node and Python)
12.3.1 Why this exists
Phase 1 prefers a split where Node handles real-time media/streaming concerns and Python handles the rest of the backend logic. Regardless of the final split, internal components must authenticate to each other so that a compromised internal endpoint cannot be used to read or modify user data.
12.3.2 Service identities (service accounts)
Define a service identity per internal component, for example:
                              * svc-node-media
                              * svc-python-api
                              * svc-worker (if a separate worker container exists)
Each service identity has:
                              * A stable service name
                              * A set of allowed internal scopes (capabilities)
12.3.3 Token-based service auth (recommended Phase 1 approach)
Use short-lived signed tokens for service-to-service calls.
Mechanism
                              * Each internal request includes Authorization: Bearer <service_token>.
                              * Service tokens are JWTs signed with a shared secret (INTERNAL_JWT_SECRET) or with an internal keypair.
Minimum JWT claims
                              * iss: issuing service (example: svc-node-media)
                              * sub: same as iss (service identity)
                              * aud: target service (example: svc-python-api)
                              * iat: issued-at time
                              * exp: short TTL (example: 5 minutes)
                              * jti: unique token ID (for replay defense if logged)
                              * scope: list of internal scopes (example: calls.write, calls.readmeta)
Validation rules
                              * Validate signature and aud.
                              * Reject expired tokens.
                              * Enforce scope checks per endpoint.
                              * Log jti and iss for traceability (without logging user content).
12.3.4 Scope design for internal endpoints
Define internal scopes aligned to real workflows, for example:
                              * calls.create
                              * calls.update_status
                              * transcripts.attach
                              * summaries.attach
                              * recordings.attach
                              * notifications.enqueue
                              * handoff.update
Internal endpoints must reject tokens without the required scope.
12.3.5 Network-level constraints (defense in depth)
Even with service tokens, internal endpoints should be network-restricted:
                              * Bind internal APIs to private interfaces only.
                              * Allow inbound only from known internal security groups/subnets (enforced at the network layer).
                              * Do not expose internal endpoints on the public internet.
(Full network policy details belong in the AWS network architecture chapter, but these constraints are required assumptions for the service-auth design.)
12.3.6 Secrets handling for internal auth (cost-sensitive, Phase 1)
Because a managed secrets manager cost is a concern, Phase 1 secrets handling direction is:
                              * Local development: .env files that are gitignored and never committed.
                              * CI/CD: GitHub Secrets to inject production secrets at deploy time.
Rules:
                              * Never embed secrets in mobile builds.
                              * Rotate internal secrets by redeploying with a new value and expiring old tokens quickly (short TTL reduces rotation risk).
12.3.7 Service-to-service authorization and user context
Internal calls often act “for a user” (example: Node receives a Twilio call event and asks Python to create/update a call record for the correct user).
Required rule:
                              * Service identity authorizes the service.
                              * User ownership is still enforced at the Python data layer by mapping the event to the correct owner_user_id (never trusting an arbitrary user_id provided by the calling service without verification).
12.3.8 Verification requirements
To prove internal auth is correct:
                              * A request to internal endpoints without a valid service token must fail.
                              * A request with a valid token but missing required scope must fail.
                              * A token for the wrong aud must fail.
                              * Replay attempts within the TTL should be detectable via logs and correlation IDs (and can be hardened later with a jti cache if needed).
________________


According to a document from 2026.
Chapter 13: Data model overview
13.1 Entity list and relationships
This chapter defines the Phase 1 core entities and how they relate, so Chapter 14 can specify tables, constraints, indexes, and migrations without missing objects that must exist for the product to work.
13.1.1 Identity and access entities
User (Account)
                              * Represents one personal user (Phase 1 target user type).
                              * Owns all data in Phase 1 via strict per-user isolation rules (every stored object is owned by exactly one user, unless explicitly marked as internal-only admin metadata).
                              * Primary relationships:
                              * User 1-to-many Devices
                              * User 1-to-many Sessions
                              * User 1-to-many Settings bundles
                              * User 1-to-many Numbers (dedicated AI number mappings)
                              * User 1-to-many Call records
                              * User 1-to-many Lists (VIP, blocked, spam)
                              * User 1-to-many Reminders
                              * User 1-to-many Memory items
                              * User 1-to-many Audit events (scoped to that user)
Device
                              * Represents a single physical device install for a user (multi-device is allowed).
                              * Stores device metadata needed for security and notifications:
                              * platform (iOS, Android)
                              * push token(s)
                              * device name (user-friendly)
                              * last_seen_at
                              * revoked_at (if removed)
                              * Relationship:
                              * Device belongs to exactly one User
                              * Device 1-to-many Sessions
                              * Device revocation must trigger local cache clearing behavior on next sync for sensitive cached content.
Session
                              * Represents an authenticated session, ideally scoped per device.
                              * Tracks:
                              * issued_at, expires_at, last_used_at
                              * revoked_at and revoke_reason
                              * Relationship:
                              * Session belongs to one User and one Device (preferred for predictable multi-device security)
Admin portal user (internal)
                              * Separate identity namespace from consumer users.
                              * Role-based access control for internal portal:
                              * Admin, Support, Viewer (minimum)
